Herein lies the beauty of exposing the path segments list and query arguments dictionary directly and providing simple add(), set(), and remove() methods for manipulation - there exist a number of intuitive ways to accomplish up(), clear(), and replace().

For up()

```python
>>> f = furl('http://www.google.com/one/two/three')
>>> f.path.segments.pop()
'three'
>>> f.url
'http://www.google.com/one/two'
```

For clear()

```python
>>> f = furl('http://www.google.com?one=1&two=2')
>>> f.args.clear()
>>> f.url
'http://www.google.com'
```

For replace()

```python
>>> f = furl('http://www.google.com?one=1&two=2')
>>> f.args['one'] = 'new1'
>>> f.url
'http://www.google.com?two=2&one=new1'
```

Multiple query arguments aren't currently supported (https://github.com/gruns/furl/blob/master/furl/furl.py#L142) but they will be soon as an OrderedMultiDic (https://github.com/gruns/furl/issues/3). Once supported, one simple way to replace() will be

```python
>>> f = furl('http://www.google.com?one=1&two=2')
>>> f.remove(['one']).add({'one':'new1'}).url
'http://www.google.com?two=2&one=new1'
```

Furl currently has no direct equivalent of click(), taking an href as input. If such becomes a commonly requested method, it may be worth discussing.

This implementation looks like it's for German banks. Other countries format the description differently. 

See "Codes used in field 86" on page 10 for various country specific codes:
https://www.handelsbanken.fi/shb/inet/icentsv.nsf/vlookuppics/a_filmformatbeskrivningar_fil_mt940_account_statement_20081212/$file/mt940_account_statement.pdf

See "Structured Field 86" on page 7 and examples in Appendix C on page 13 for how it's formatted in the Netherlands:
https://www.staalbankiers.nl/media/124389/mt940%20description%20v1.1.pdf

It could be included specifically as the German variant but not added by default. Other variants could be added and maybe some common code could be reused. There might even be an 'auto-detect' version that could be added at some point.

The `glad2` branch now supports -pedantic (and all the other flags).

Support is achieved almost fully by using function pointers instead of `void*`, currently only in two places casts from function pointers to `void*` (or the other way round) are necessary:

1) within the utility loader function which stores the user loader function within the `void*` userpointer
2) within the internal loader (`dlsym`)

To still be able to compile with -pedantic all remaining casts are marked with
[`__extension__`](https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc/Alternate-Keywords.html#Alternate-Keywords
) for `__GNUC__` compilers (e.g. GCC and clang).

...Though on further that actually, that does leave a tricky question about what to do with code like:

```python
async with timeout_after(5):
        async with timeout_after(10):
            try:
                await curio.sleep(100)         # after 5 seconds, outer timeout expires
            finally:
                # while unwinding the outer timeout, we yield and the *inner* timeout fires
                await curio.sleep(100)
```

This could get nasty, because in the naive implementation where all timeouts fire when ready, then the inner block's exception could fire second and end up overwriting the outer block's exception. Which would be bad -- the whole point of `timeout_after` is that it's supposed to be able to impose a timeout on a whole block, so once the outer `timeout_after` has fired then that should take priority over whatever internal timeouts the block might contain.

I think this means that the right semantics are: whenever an "outer" `timeout_after` fires, we disarm all the `timeout_after` blocks inside it. So in a case like the above, the inner `timeout_after` is effectively a no-op, and this is obvious from the start. But this also can arise in cases where multiple timeouts fire simultaneously:

```python
async with timeout_after(10):
    async with timeout_after(5):
        # note: *not* curio.sleep, simulating a coroutine that doesn't enter the kernel for a while
        time.sleep(20)
        # now when we enter the kernel and trigger a timeout check, both timeouts have expired
        await curio.sleep(1)
```

Here it was possible in principle for the inner timeout to fire first, but as things turned out they fired simultaneously, so the outer one should override the inner one.

Summing up, I think something like this gives the desired semantics. One nice thing is that it turns out that because we have ruled out inner timeouts firing after outer timeouts, we don't need to tag exceptions with unique cookies after all -- a simple nesting depth suffices:

```python
# logic somewhere inside the kernel used for firing timeouts
if now > task._deadline_stack[-1]:
    # some timeout is firing, but which?
    nesting_depth = 0
    while now > task._deadline_stack[-1]:
        nesting_depth += 1
        task._deadline_stack.pop()
exc = CancelledDueToTimeout()
exc._nesting_depth = nesting_depth
coro.throw(exc)

class timeout_after:
    async def __aenter__(self):
        task = await curio.current_task()
        deadline_stack = task._deadline_stack
        # hack: if our deadline is too far in the future, it can never fire, but it's simplest if we always
        # push something onto the stack. So we duplicate the topmost deadline, and then if/when
        # we fire then the timeout below us will also fire at the same time, and override us.
        deadline = self.deadline
        if deadline_stack[-1] < self.deadline:
            deadline = deadline_stack[-1]
        deadline_stack.append(deadline)

    async def __aexit__(self, type, exc, traceback):
        if isinstance(exc, CancelledDueToTimeout):
            exc._nesting_depth -= 1
            if exc._nesting_depth == 0:
                # we're the timeout_after that triggered this cancellation, and the code we
                # were wrapping has all been canceled. So convert to a timeout exception.
                raise CurioTimeoutError from exc
        # otherwise, let the exception continue propagating (possibly with reduced _nesting_depth)
```

To be sure, the GC of async generators is less than ideal.   How much leverage do you want to have in pushing for PEP 533?   I'd be perfectly fine in having Curio take a hard-line and do nothing more than issue warning messages. Maybe even linking to the PEP in the warning. 

That said, if its not going to be fixed, there are things that could probably be done in Curio. For example, associating async generators with the task in which they were started and forcing them closed when the task exits.   Yes, it would prevent things such as passing an already started generator to another task.  However, in thinking about that, I don't think I've ever even passed an already started generator to another function let alone another task/thread in my whole experience of using generators.   So, it's not going to bother me to disallow that.

@hgrecco Your suggestion to define log units in a different way looks good. I will change the `Definition.from_string()` parser correspondingly. However, I am not sure which values the resulting Definition instance should contain. Look at three definition examples:
```
x1 = Definition.from_string('dBm = 0.001 * watt; logbase: 10; factor: 10')   # correct definition
self.assertEqual(x1.converter.scale, 0.001)  # this factor is used by LogConverter
self.assertEqual(x1.converter.logbase, 10)
self.assertEqual(x1.converter.factor, 10)
self.assertEqual(x1.reference, UnitsContainer(watt=1))  # this unit is ignored during conversion

x2 = Definition.from_string('dBm = watt; logbase: 10; factor: 10')   # incorrect definition, 1e-3 missing
self.assertEqual(x2.converter.scale, 1)  # this factor is used by LogConverter
self.assertEqual(x2.converter.logbase, 10)
self.assertEqual(x2.converter.factor, 10)
self.assertEqual(x2.reference, UnitsContainer(watt=1))  # this unit is ignored during conversion

x3 = Definition.from_string('dBm = milliwatt; logbase: 10; factor: 10')  # correct definition
self.assertEqual(x3.converter.scale, 1)    # this factor is used by LogConverter
self.assertEqual(x3.converter.logbase, 10)
self.assertEqual(x3.converter.factor, 10)
self.assertEqual(x3.reference, UnitsContainer(milliwatt=1))   # this unit is ignored during conversion
```
x1 and x3 are defined from correct strings, while x2 is not. The conversion is correct with x2 and x3 however! The unit of reference is not taken into account. I will have a further look if 
> The root unit will be taken from the reference.

After a brief look it seemed to me that NonMultiplicativeRegistry._convert() considers 'dBm' as an offset unit (or nonmultiplicative), and therefore conversion is done from watt to dimensionless, i.e. from watt to  1000 * gram * meter ** 2 / second ** 3, and not from watt to reference. May be i am missing smth. :)

Hi @facetoe I was trying to update a custom_field_options from a TicketField, and with the CRUD I wasnt able to update it, but this is a current bug on Zendesk with custom_field_options.
For exemple, this is a ticket_fields.to_dict() printable:

{
  "active": true,
  "collapsed_for_agents": false,
  "created_at": "2017-10-17T09:33:35Z",
  "description": "",
  "editable_in_portal": false,
  "id": 114100270111,
  "position": 9999,
  "raw_description": "",
  "raw_title": "Modelo",
  "raw_title_in_portal": "Modelo",
  "required": false,
  "required_in_portal": false,
  "title": "Modelo",
  "title_in_portal": "Modelo",
  "type": "tagger",
  "updated_at": "2017-12-20T09:03:08Z",
  "url": "HERE WAS AN URL",
  "visible_in_portal": false,
  "removable": true,
  "custom_field_options": [
    {
      "id": 114096469691,
      "name": "sergi",
      "raw_name": "sergi",
      "value": "mans_not_hot",
      "default": false
    },
    {
      "id": 114096279152,
      "name": "test_3",
      "raw_name": "test_3",
      "value": "test_update_3",
      "default": false
    },
  ]
}

And you want to append {"name": "test_new", "value": "test_not_hot"} in custom_field_options, it would be something like:

    ticket_field = zenpy.ticket_fields(id=TICKET_FIELD_ID)
    ticket_field.custom_field_options.append({"name": "test_new", "value": "test_not_hot"})
    zenpy.ticket_fields.update(ticket_field)

But the update wont be made, same with zendesk, if you curl ticket_field.to_dict() it wont made nothing, so to fix it, you need to be specific and just send {"ticket_fields": {"custom_field_options"}}

In zenpy it looks like:

    from zenpy.lib.api_objects import TicketField

    ticket_field = zenpy.ticket_fields(id=TICKET_FIELD_ID)
    ticket_field.custom_field_options.append({"name": "test_new", "value": "test_not_hot"})
    zenpy.ticket_fields.update(TicketField(id=ticket_field.id, custom_field_options=ticket_field.custom_field_options))

And this will made the update

Due to inheritance, *most* adapters will be an instance of `HTTPAdapter`. If you do 

```py
class Foo(HTTPAdapter):
    pass

f = Foo()
print(isinstance(f, HTTPAdapter))
```

You'll see `True`.

You need ISO 8601 dates: https://developer.zendesk.com/rest_api/docs/help_center/introduction

`date.isoformat( )` or `datetime.isoformat( )` should work.

The python datetime does not have the "Z" (as in Zulu) suffix and don't have a timezone, but with a bit of chance you don't need it. If you do then have a look at http://stackoverflow.com/a/23705687 which lists how you can get the UTC ISO date format with timezone offset.

Yes, promgen is the right thing to use here, but that only solves half the problem. When the CPU boots up it will execute the instructions in the bootrom (https://github.com/openrisc/orpsoc-cores/blob/master/systems/atlys/rtl/verilog/rom.v#L58) which will just put it in an endless loop. You need to replace the contents of this boot rom with instructions that gets the data out from the SPI flash. There is probably something hidden deep inside of orpsocv2 that you can use, bit it's a bit messy.

As a side note, I'm working on a collection of useful bootloaders that I'm going to add to orpsoc-cores soon that can be used for any board. I'm planning to put a SPI Flash loader there once I have dug up the one from orpsocv2 and polished it up a bit. I'm afraid that is no help for you right now, but I wanted to let you know that we at least are working on better solutions for these kinds of problems

And it seems that this functionality is built-in in ```nbconvert``` now.(see #54).

it was pretty straight forward - I had an automation rule that when run simply calls homeassistant.turnoff as follows:

`- alias: Away Mode Activated
  hide_entity: true
  trigger:
    platform: state
    entity_id: input_select.home_mode
    # Optional
    from: 'Home'
    to: 'Away'

    # Alias for 'to'
    state: 'Away'

    # If given, will trigger when state has been the to state for X time.
    #for:
    #  hours: 0
    #  minutes: 5
    #  seconds: 0
  #condition:
  #  condition: state
  #  entity_id: input_select.home_mode
  #  state: 'Away'
  action:
    - service: homeassistant.turn_off
      entity_id: light.livingroom_light
    - service: homeassistant.turn_off
      entity_id: light.stairs_light
`
(plus more switches/lights)

Basically, when this automation runs - all devices in the list toggle rather than turn off, so if they're off, they toggle on.   This then results in the device in HA being out of step with Vera - i.e. vera is showing the right state, but HA shows the wrong state.

For Example
LivingRoom Light on Stairs Light off (Both correct in Vera & HA)
Active the automation - the living room light turns off.
In VERA UI - both are off.
In HA UI - Living Room Light is off, Stairs Light is on.

If the device is already off in HA and the command issued from the automation, then the switch in the HA interface toggles on, but the off command to vera is issued (so if I turn on the device device manually, it will turn off even though HA shows it as on). 

At that point, they're out of step with the Vera interface, and the only way to resurrect is to either toggle on in vera to match the state of HA toggles, or to restart HA and it'll re-pickup the correct status from vera again.

That make sense?

HI @etingof, can you check the following code? It's almost identical to the original patch posted by @tmarlok88:

```
from pyasn1.codec.ber.encoder import AbstractItemEncoder, encode, tagMap, typeMap
from pyasn1.type import univ

class LDAPBooleanEncoder(AbstractItemEncoder):
    supportIndefLenMode = False

    def encodeValue(self, value, asn1Spec, encodeFun, **options):
        substrate = (0,) if value == 0 else (255,)
        return substrate, False, False

tagMap[univ.Boolean.tagSet] = LDAPBooleanEncoder()
typeMap[univ.Boolean.typeId] = LDAPBooleanEncoder()

print(encode(univ.Boolean(True)))
```
Or do you think that a new Encoder() should be created explicitly with modified copy of tagMap and typeMap:

```
from pyasn1.codec.ber.encoder import AbstractItemEncoder, Encoder, tagMap, typeMap
from pyasn1.type import univ
from copy import deepcopy

class LDAPBooleanEncoder(AbstractItemEncoder):
    supportIndefLenMode = False

    def encodeValue(self, value, asn1Spec, encodeFun, **options):
        substrate = (0,) if value == 0 else (255,)
        return substrate, False, False

customTagMap = deepcopy(tagMap)
customTypeMap = deepcopy(typeMap)
customTagMap[univ.Boolean.tagSet] = LDAPBooleanEncoder()
customTypeMap[univ.Boolean.typeId] = LDAPBooleanEncoder()

encode = Encoder(customTagMap, customTypeMap)
print(encode(univ.Boolean(True)))
```

The second solution don't change the original pyasn1 behaviour so probably could be used with older and newer version of pyasn1.

I was afraid this was gonna come up at some point.
Unfortunately, this issue is closely tied to the fundamental philosophy of this project: ease of use.
Tkinter only supports one `tk.Tk` instance at runtime (it is tied to a global Tcl interpreter). To create new windows, you're supposed to instantiate a `tk.Toplevel` object:

```python
import tkinter as tk

root = tk.Tk()
window_2 = tk.Toplevel(root)
```

But `App.__init__` creates unconditionally creates a new `tk.Tk` object.

---

So, what now?
There's a couple of things we could do:

1. Change the documentation to mention that only one `App` object may be alive at a given time. In this case, we should probably change the implementation too, and print an error message in case an attempt is made to create a second instance. Perhaps not feasible, because there is an apparent need for secondary windows.

2. Change the implementation of `App` to create either a `tk.Tk` instance or a `tk.Toplevel` instance, depending on whether or not a root window already exists. Added complexity: lots of error handling.

3. Add a `Window` type with much of the same functionality (through mixins) as `App`.

I really got to write some documentation on how to implement the tests. So let's explain it a bit here, so I'll have something stashed for when I'll write the doc ☺

Basically, git-repo has two layers:

* `git_repo.repo`: is where all the command line arguments are parsed, the service instanciated and is user of the service API
* `git_repo.services`: defines the services
 * `git_repo.services.service`: which contains: 
  * the base class for each specialized service,  
  * common methods that are service agnostic,
  * contains the entry point for the service selection mechanism.
 * `git_repo.services.ext.*`: which contains each specialized service (and registers it)

So the tests are actually matching that distinction:

* [`tests.integration.test_main`](https://github.com/guyzmo/git-repo/blob/master/tests/integration/test_main.py): 

it tests `git_repo.repo` stuff. What that mean is that it mocks up the input argument dictionary (that you can see when you call `git-repo -vvvvv`), using [a helper method](https://github.com/guyzmo/git-repo/blob/master/tests/helpers.py#L264,L270) run the call against it and exposes what the `git_repo.services.service` based API would receive as arguments, and then specializes the test using a [test case in `tests.integration.test_main`](https://github.com/guyzmo/git-repo/blob/master/tests/integration/test_main.py#L86-L88).

As a code is worth 1000 words, the test for your change could be done by calling `clone`:

``` python
    def test_clone(self):
        repo_slug_branch, seen_args = self.main_clone('guyzmo/test.git.test', 0)
        assert ('guyzmo', 'test.git.test', 'master') == repo_slug_branch
```

So as you can see, we're only testing the first layer, and thus there is no need for the repository to exist. There are some actions that run the `git` command though to create a temporary git repository to test things like guessing names based on the repository.

* [`tests.integration.test_(github|gitlab|bitbucket)`](https://github.com/guyzmo/git-repo/blob/master/tests/integration/test_github.py)

There the tests only starts at the services API (so we don't care about what's being done at the `git_repo.repo` level), and it needs to work against the real API.

But hopefully, you need to setup the *real* conditions for the test only once. Thanks to [betamax](https://github.com/sigmavirus24/betamax), when the `tests/integration/cassettes/*.json` file matching the test is missing, the test case will run the real HTTP interaction and record them as a JSON file that gets stored there. If the json file exists, then it will play the recorded version and no HTTP request will be sent by the test runner.

This is actually a really good idea, especially since I'm very hesitant/lazy about documenting things or writing text in general.

Regarding your question:
Directory- and filenames are built by applying a key/value dictionary to a [format string](https://docs.python.org/3/library/string.html#formatstrings) like `"{artist-id}-{artist-nick}"` (For directories this is a list of format strings: one for each path segment, because slash/backslash separators for unix/windows) .
This dictionary gets generated by the respective extractor and contains a bunch of key-value pairs, which you can inspect by using the `--list-keywords` option:
```bash
$ gallery-dl --list-keywords http://www.pixiv.net/member_illust.php?id=11
Keywords for directory names:
artist-id:   11
artist-name: pixiv事務局
artist-nick: pixiv
category:    pixiv
subcategory: user

Keywords for filenames:
age_limit:       all-age
artist-id:       11
artist-name:     pixiv事務局
artist-nick:     pixiv
book_style:      right_to_left
...
category:        pixiv
content_type:    None
created_time:    2017-03-31 13:50:53
extension:       jpg
favorite_id:     0
height:          865
id:              62178245
...
```
Every extractor always supplies the `category` and `subcategory` key for each dictionary.
Each extractor also has a default format string for directory- and filenames. You've already dug up the one for pixiv: `["{category}", "{artist-id}-{artist-nick}"]`.
These can be overwritten in your config-file by setting the `directory` or `filename` value for an extractor, like so
```json
{
  "extractor": {
    "pixiv": {
      "directory": ["my pixiv images", "{artist-id}"],
      "filename": "{created_time:}.{extension}"
    }
  }
}
```
This will result in paths like `...\my pixiv images\11\2017-03-31 13:50:53.jpg`.
To conclude: The `category` value can be used to specify an extractor in your config file and is the first component of every default directory format string.

```python
bot = telepot.Bot('TOKEN')

and 
```python
bot = telepot.Bot('TOKEN')

bot.sendDocument(1234567, ("後____________________________________________________________________________壞", output_file))

In both of the cases, the filename of receive side is "unnamed.file".

I just hit the same issue.  Really the best way to fix this would be to modify ebooklib.utils#parse_string so that it accepts some kwargs.  Then the user can specify a parser if they require one.

I ended up patching my local install of ebooklib so that it used my version of the parse_string function that ebooklib calls in the _load_container() method.

My modified "parse_string" method is as follows:

```
def parse_string(s):
    parser = etree.XMLParser(remove_comments=True)
    try:
        tree = etree.parse(io.BytesIO(s.encode('utf-8')), parser=parser)
    except:
        tree = etree.parse(io.BytesIO(s), parser=parser)
    return tree
```

I then just patched my local copy so that instead of importing parse_string from ebooklib.utils I imported it from mymodule.utils

> From the error message maybe the server just doesn't like the closing tags?

Possibly, possibly.  Look at the OFX header; we're sending version 102 - that is SGML, not strict XML.  Closing tags are optional (but legal).  Try sending version 2xx (e.g. 203)... that is strict XML which must have closing tags.  If they won't accept XML they should send an error message to that effect.  If they do accept OFXv2, they damned well better parse closing tags!

If they will only take unclosed tags, and won't accept closing tags for OFXv1, that's a violation of the spec.

From my limited understanding this is what happens:
First the code in question:
```
    def _fire_pending_completed_requests(self):
        responses = []
        while self._pending_completion:
            thread = threading.current_thread()
            response, future = self._pending_completion.popleft()
            future.success(response)
            responses.append(response)
        return responses
```
Thread 1 and 2 are the main thread and the heartbeat thread. However the roles of both are interchangable.
1) Both threads hit _fire_pending_completed_requests and process self._pending_completion until one item is left
2) Now thread 1 hits the conditional in the while loop again and gets back True
3) Thread 2 gets scheduled and hits the conditional and gets back True
4) One of them continues and empties self._pending_completion
5) The other tries to popleft and triggers the exception

After building Qt5, you should be able to continue with the usual instructions using make qt5py3 (if you check the Makefile it doesn't specify the Python version for that instruction). # After installing qt5 for python 2.7
sudo pip install lxml

# Using the Makefile

# If that doesn't work, specify python version

I guess I got the same or a similar problem with a memory leak produced by tablesorter.
I am using tablesorter for a single page application and often remove and append tables.

Some months ago I noticed that the number of nodes in the timeline was doubling for each removal and appending of a table of nearly the same size.

(The short spikes are intended, but right before them there should be the deletion of the old nodes, I already checked that this spikes have nothing to do with the memory leak)
![tablesortermemoryleak](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/9080610/13269193/c954894c-da86-11e5-8e48-784f09164573.png)


Over quite a time I tried to find a solution for this but finally I decided to tear apart my application until I find the origin of the leak.

Well, as you can already imagine: this part was tablesorter, the pure tablesorter init via  $(table).tablesorter({});
After I removed this line of code the memory leak was gone and didn't occur after some removals and appendings.

I have to add here that I have the following lines in my code for destroying the tablesorter and clearing its parent object before the new table is appended.
   $(document.getElementById(table)).trigger("destroy");
   $(document.getElementById("parent" + table)).empty());

Actually this two lines do destroy the tablesorter instance and remove the objects from the DOM, but they have no impact on the nodes displaying in the Timeline (Chome dev console).

So I guess that something still references to the nodes as the GC doesn't clean up the nodes if tablesorter was previously attached to the table.

I already tried finding the reference by looking through several Heap Snapshots and Heap Allocations but I couldn't find the reference or the references that kept the tables from being processed by the GC.

I hope that this report may help at finding this memory leak and I will for sure take a look at the tablesorter code myself when I find the time for it in the more or less near future.

(BTW: You did a great job with this fork, really appreciate it!)

At some point, I had to generate UUIDs as well, and I must have encountered the uniqueness issue you mention, because I wrote (copied from somewhere more likely) the following as a replacement:
```python
from uuid import getnode as getMAC
import os
import time
import random


def getHostIDComponent():
    """
    Uses the mac address to generate the "HostID" 
    component for creating UUIDs
    """
    mac = getMAC()
    if (mac >> 40) % 2:
        raise OSError, "No MAC Address found"
    macBytes = [(mac >> i) & 255 for i in range(0,41,8)] 
    oredBytes = [l[0] ^ l[1] for l in zip(macBytes[0:3],macBytes[3:6])]  
    val = oredBytes[0]  *256*256 + oredBytes[1] *256 +oredBytes[2]
    return val
    
def getProcessIDComponent():
    """
    Just returns the processID
    """
    return os.getpid()
    
def getTimeComponent():
    """
    Just returns the current time as an int
    """    
    return int(time.time())

def getCounterGen():
    """
    This is the sequential counter (per process) 
    to guarantee uniqueness even when called in 
    rapid succesion
    """    
    num = random.randint(0,100)
    while True:
        yield num
        num += 1
# a global pointer to a generator (above)
getCounter = getCounterGen()

def test_generateInstanceUID():
    assert(generateInstanceUID(PREFIX = "1") != generateInstanceUID(PREFIX = "1"))
    
def generateInstanceUID(PREFIX = ""):
    """
    This is the only public member here
    """
    return "{}.{}.{}.{}.{}".format(PREFIX,getHostIDComponent(),getProcessIDComponent(),getTimeComponent(),getCounter.next())
```

Hi @cwood32 

There are two problems with this particular line. 

a) the log line is too long, and has the `warning: log line attempted...` string at the beginning. Therefore mtools doesn't recognize it as a typical operation. There is an open request to fix that (and ignore the warning), see #133. I should really fix that soon. 

b) this particular line is actually a "getmore", which is a request for another batch after the initial query. These are tracked as separate operations. So even if the line wasn't too long, it would not show up as query. If you want to include these in your filter you'd have to run something like this:

```
python mlogfilter.py c:\mongodb.log --operation query getmore --namespace WickedWorkload_2.Patient --slow 1000
```

I hope this answers your question, and I will schedule #133 to be included for the next release. 

Thomas

Just to follow up, the pattern matching should allow for some special values, from page 673 of the 2012 standard:

    Date ::= [APPLICATION 10] OCTET STRING (SIZE(4)) -- see 20.2.12
    -- first octet     year minus 1900    X'FF' = unspecified
    -- second octet    month (1..14)      1 = January
                                          13 = odd months
                                          14 = even months
                                          X'FF' = unspecified
    -- third octet     day of month (1..34)
                                          32 = last day of month
                                          33 = odd days of month
                                          34 = even days of month
                                          X'FF' = unspecified
    -- fourth octet    day of week (1..7)
                                          1 = Monday
                                          7 = Sunday
                                          X'FF' = unspecified

No doubt there are many combinations of these special values that are meaningless, and some that are disallowed in some context but allowed in others, and some that are going to be ambiguous.

As for using https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_8601, since it doesn't support wildcard characters or day-of-week, that won't work well.  But making **str()** be more universal like "yyyy-mm-dd dow", I'm all for that.

Your description is missing network numbers, so I'm going to assume that your PC is 192.168.0.3 on BACnet network 2, and your router routes between 192.168.0.4 on network 2 and address 99 on network 1.  Your mission is to send a read request to address 1 on network 1.

If you are running the `ReadProperty.py` application and just say `read 1:1 device 20 objectName` it is going to attempt to find the router to network 1, which you mention is blocked (probably because reading a property uses a confirmed service, and confirmed services shouldn't be broadcast).  There is a `rtn` command which you can use to tell the network layer of your application where to find the router to the destination network, `rtn 192.168.0.4 1` will add that into the local routing tables cache.

After that your read will "magically" become a unicast message on the BACnet/IP network, and then be a unicast message on the LONtalk network.

The final implementation respects the original implementation by *Ehlers* and rather than expecting the price component to have `high` and `low` it takes simply `self.data` (which unless changed is the `close` from regular data feeds)

Using it for the midpoint of the bar is easy:

```python
midpoint = (self.data.high + self.data.low) / 2.0
lrsi = bt.ind.LRSI(midpoint)
```

The advantange of not having specifics like `high` and `low` in the code is that the `LRSI` can be calculated for anything which is a data feed or descendant of ... you could create the `LRSI` of a `SimpleMovingAverage`

Tested with the IB data feed and it was actually breaking with 2 datas due to how the data loading was kickstarted.  Reordering the kickstarting has not proven to be a real challenge and things work.

But the *master* vs *slave* issue kicks in. Due to the blocking part if a data has low liquidity and the timeframe is small, the slave data may become out of sync with the main data. This is because the two datas will produce data that will be resampled into different periods.

Example:
  - The *master* data produces a "1 second" bar after 3 seconds.
  - The *slave* data produces 3 "1 second" bars after 3 seconds

The system asks the *master* to return a data and if available, asks the *slave* to return a data. But this data is the 1st of the 3 bars it has generated. And this is already behind the *master* which due to the low liquidity has taken 3 seconds to generate the 1st 1 second bar.

See the following quick example:

```
$ ./ibtest.py --port 7497 --data0 TWTR --data1 AAPL-STK-SMART-USD --resample --timeframe Seconds --compression 1 --no-backfill_start
Server Version: 76
TWS Time at connection:20160801 22:20:30 CET
--------------------------------------------------
Strategy Created
--------------------------------------------------
Timezone from ContractDetails: EST5EDT
Datetime, Open, High, Low, Close, Volume, OpenInterest, SMA
***** STORE NOTIF: <error id=-1, errorCode=2104, errorMsg=Market data farm connection is OK:ibdemo>
***** STORE NOTIF: <error id=-1, errorCode=2106, errorMsg=HMDS data farm connection is OK:demohmds>
***** DATA NOTIF: CONNECTED
***** DATA NOTIF: DELAYED
***** DATA NOTIF: CONNECTED
***** DATA NOTIF: DELAYED
***** DATA NOTIF: LIVE
***** DATA NOTIF: LIVE
Data0, 0001, 2016-08-01T16:20:31.000000, 16.6, 16.6, 16.6, 16.6, 353.0, 0, nan
Data1, 0001, 2016-08-01T16:20:31.000000, 104.54, 104.54, 104.54, 104.54, 85.0, 0, nan
Data0, 0002, 2016-08-01T16:20:32.000000, 16.6, 16.6, 16.6, 16.6, 15.0, 0, nan
Data1, 0002, 2016-08-01T16:20:32.000000, 104.56, 104.56, 104.56, 104.56, 1.0, 0, nan
Data0, 0003, 2016-08-01T16:20:33.000000, 16.61, 16.61, 16.61, 16.61, 3.0, 0, nan
Data1, 0002, 2016-08-01T16:20:32.000000, 104.56, 104.56, 104.56, 104.56, 1.0, 0, nan
Data0, 0004, 2016-08-01T16:20:34.000000, 16.6, 16.6, 16.6, 16.6, 2.0, 0, nan
Data1, 0003, 2016-08-01T16:20:34.000000, 104.55, 104.55, 104.55, 104.55, 9.0, 0, nan
```

Already at `16:20:33` sync is lost. In this case the *slave* has also undergone a period of low liquidity and it re-syncs 1 second later. But it could have remained out of sync forever, even increasing the difference.

But this is a false sync. Notice how the "length" of the datas is different in the las 2 line:

  - `0004` vs `0003`

If the loss of sync happened because the slave were always producing more bars, the problem could be solved by merging all available bars into a single one. But the problem when the slave produces too few bars cannot be solved, because there is no data.

The sample run above uses a small timeframe of `Seconds 1`. In larger timeframes, but still small in absolute terms, like 30 seconds, 1 minutes is unlikely to be seen unless the assets really have a very low low liquidity.

With the changes in place and testing against the "demo" of IB, which has low liquidity for everything, no out-of-sync sequences have been seen. But here you rmileage may vary always depending on your target timeframe and asset liquidty.

It seems like you underestimate the role of exceptions in Python. They are a very normal idiom in Python, and are even used for flow control, including the Python programmers themselves. For example, the end of EVERY container loop is indicated by a StopIteration exception which is then caught by the looping code (internally) to end the loop. A segfault however is a C-based problem so serious that it even cannot be caught by exceptions, so your expectation is kinda off there. Exceptions are very much thought to be used for user errors and prevent hidden bugs by the user forgetting to check old language style things like return codes. Imagine the result is being used only much further down the line, it would be much harder to find where the problem was created. In the case of `bod2nc` there is no other reasonable result as to get the result of the conversion. If for some reason that conversion fails, a very important SPICE rule, which is to have the required kernels loaded at this point, was not followed. As a SPICE user I think I would want to know immediately that I forgot to have kernels loaded at this point.

The issue with swagger-ui is that deployment uses HTML files and each new version needs a merge in the template here in flask-apispec. ReDoc is easier to embed as it just takes a line in the template to refer to the JS file. See [our template](https://github.com/Nobatek/flask-rest-api/blob/master/flask_rest_api/spec/templates/redoc.html#L20):

```html
  <body>
    <redoc spec-url="{{ url_for('api-docs.openapi_json') }}" lazy-rendering></redoc>
    <script src="https://rebilly.github.io/ReDoc/releases/latest/redoc.min.js"> </script>
  </body>
```

About the use cases...

I used to develop USB HID/Audio composite devices firmware, so this library started long time ago as a tool helper.

This explains the native HID reports/usages emphasis in the API. Windows provides a nice report abstraction for applications, so in theory, devices could avoid using raw reports for better compatibility.

In practice, firmware developers lack the proper HID knowledge and existing tools usability don't quite help to build report descriptors (the USB HID report tools is kind of a joke usability wise), and seems most interest about the library is centered about using raw reports.

That's the reason I build the helper example scripts and provided raw reports management facilities.

I still use the library in my engineering tools, and some important user feedback falls into "helper for firmware/device" developers items, so as a firmware developer I don't usually agree on patching the library just to cope with a rough device.

For scenarios where a specific device breaks the library, if a firmware patch is not possible, an application patch is expected (e.g. as long as an exception is raised in the library, I think this should be fine).

In your case, I thought the patch was valid, the fixed 50mS delay is an ugly hack, but used to work.

Just let me get all my test devices from my drawers, get a little bit of time and I think your patches will be merged, don't worry.

Yeah, although if the rate of client allocations is low enough, the chances of a collision should be too low to worry about.

With just one (central) rendezvous server, allocations are sound (there's no way for two clients to be allocated the same value) regardless of how fast they arrive. If you aren't on the internet, so you're running a rendezvous server yourself, then you might overlap with someone else who's doing the same thing (running `wormhole send` at the same time as you), and it'd be good to avoid collisions. The "pre-flight" check would be to query all the (MDNS) servers we can find, and ask them for all the channels they're currently hosting. Then the race window opens, and we pick a channel at random, and before we reveal it to the user (in a wormhole code), we stall a little bit for other senders to query us. Say we wait one second. If someone else picked the same channel as we did, and queried us just before we picked that channel, then we'll have a collision.

Eyeballing it, the chances of a collision are the round trip time, divied by the mean inter-arrival request time, multiplied by the probability of choosing the same channel (e.g. 10% for one-digit channels). It's been a long time since I studied probability theory, though, so I wouldn't bet on my math :).

Good point. I think this is a dup of #31, but I'm glad to have the extra consideration linked to it. My plan was to have the server put the nameplates into a "reserved" list, where they'll cool down for an hour or so before becoming eligible for a third party claim.

In general, the security of the connection is completely dependent upon the security of the wormhole code, up until the point when both legitimate sides have claimed the connection (after that point, the code can be safely revealed). So if we're talking about a threat model in which the attacker gets to know the code early, then that kind of attacker is indistinguishable from the legitimate recipient (modulo after-the-fact verification-string comparisons as you noted).

So if the code isn't kept secret until that point, an attack is pretty easy. Having the server block code re-use for some period of time makes the full man-in-the-middle attack slightly harder, but:

* the attacker is still free to mount a one-sided attack, where Alice thinks that Bob got the file (but in fact she gave it to the attacker), and Bob thinks that an error occurred
* if the attacker controls the network between the legitimate parties, they might be able to block the error message that Bob sees, or the interactive "hey, that's funny, my side says we're done, but your side says we're still waiting" conversation that would happen
* the server is still able to mount a re-use attack

which is why I haven't prioritized this change yet. I've started on a branch to address it (31-reserve-channelid), but I want to analyze the attack a bit more carefully and identify exactly what this buys us (and what sorts of attacks are still possible).

In this case postmarker's Django backend works similar to ``django.code.mail.backends.smtp.EmailBackend`` (the following code is taken from this class):

```python
    def _send(self, email_message):
        # preparation 
        message = email_message.message()
        # sending with exceptions catching
```

Here, the `message` method transforms Django's ``EmailMessage`` / ``EmailMultiAlternatives`` to ``SafeMIMEText`` (an extension of ``email.mime.text.MIMEText``).
After that, message is send via concrete backend methods. In case of postmarker via ``PostmarkClient.emails.send_batch`` method.

This method works well with default Python email entities (as ``MIMEText``, ``MIMEMultipart``, etc). Basically this method converts given instances to postmarker's ``Email`` instances via ``Email.from_mime`` method.

So, I can propose to do this - create a new class, derived from Django's ``EmailMultiAlternatives`` and set ``tag`` attribute:

Python 3-only compatible example:

```python
class TaggedEmailMultiAlternatives(EmailMultiAlternatives):

    def __init__(self, *args, tag=None, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.tag = tag
```

After that I can implement 3rd option from my first comment and add it to the next release.

Or I can do both - add a mixin/class, which will provide such features out of the box. Probably it would be better option. In the same way it would be possible to add some more postmark-only features to Django email messages - like per-message TrackOpens or TrackLinks.

Ok, here is my use case: A small helper class for easy handling of RPC-calls using AMQP. The `ResponseMessageHandler` sends a message via `publishAndAwaitResponse`. It creates a correlation_id (uuid) and waits for a response using a future object.

```python
import asynqp
import asyncio
import uuid

class ResponseMessageHandler:
    
    def __init__(self, channel, exchange):
        self.exchange = exchange
        self.channel = channel
        self.awaitedResponses = {}
        self.queueName = None
        
        
    def consumeResponseMessage(self, receivedMessage):
        if receivedMessage.correlation_id in self.awaitedResponses:
            callback, future = self.awaitedResponses[receivedMessage.correlation_id]
            del self.awaitedResponses[receivedMessage.correlation_id]
            
            try:
                result = callback(receivedMessage)
                future.set_result(result)
            except e:
                future.set_exception(e)

    
    @asyncio.coroutine
    def publishAndAwaitResponse(self, message, routingKey, callback):
        correlationId = uuid.uuid4().hex
        message.properties['correlation_id'] = correlationId
        message.properties['reply_to'] = self.queueName
        self.exchange.publish(message, routingKey)
        
        future = asyncio.Future()
        self.awaitedResponses[correlationId] = (callback, future)
        ret = yield from future
        return ret
        
        
    @asyncio.coroutine
    def setupResponseQueue(self, queueName):
        self.queueName = queueName
        self.responseQueue = yield from self.channel.declare_queue(queueName)
        yield from self.responseQueue.bind(self.exchange, queueName)
        yield from self.responseQueue.consume(self.consumeResponseMessage)
```

Autodiscover HTTP requests already have a timeout:

https://github.com/ecederstrand/exchangelib/blob/ddd509f848ef87f9c06520e664d117944db39d63/exchangelib/autodiscover.py#L495

Autodiscovery may take a while because there could be a long list of servers to try, but it should always return at some point. Under certain failure scenarios when using a `ServiceAccount` to connect, autodiscovery make only give up after 1 hour. See the `max_wait` argument:

https://github.com/ecederstrand/exchangelib/blob/ddd509f848ef87f9c06520e664d117944db39d63/exchangelib/credentials.py#L77

However, I wouldn't suggest you to fix this project because`eth-testrpc` uses [pyethereum](https://github.com/ethereum/pyethereum) as a node, which is no longer maintained. Solidity >=0.4.22 has introduced some new opcodes which aren't implemeted in  `pyethereum`. This will give you weird errors with `pyethereum`.
` eth-tester` uses [py-evm](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm), which is the new python node and `eth-tester-rpc` just serves and rpc endpoint for `eth-tester`.

I recently ran into a similarly baffling issue with my files all being 'private' even though I was setting `AWS_DEFAULT_ACL = 'public-read'`. What had happened was that a piece of code we had copied from a blog was unintentionally setting the `default_acl` attribute on the main django file storage singleton (`django.core.files.storage.default_storage`) to 'private'. This code ran at the start of every request, which would then cause all files uploaded during requests to have the 'private' act.

So I'd suggest looking through your code to see if anything is assigning to the `default_acl` attribute on an object.

This is a problem in how Python handles decimals. I was also stuck with this until I could correctly format the values sent to the API.

Use this function to format both quantity and price, using the filters corresponding to the transaction symbol.

```
import math

def floatPrecision(self, f, n):
	n = int(math.log10(1 / float(n)))
	f = math.floor(float(f) * 10 ** n) / 10 ** n
	f = "{:0.0{}f}".format(float(f), n)
	return str(int(f)) if int(n) == 0 else f
```

An example:

```
symbol_info = client.get_symbol_info('ETHUSDT')
tick_size = float(filter(lambda f: f['filterType'] == 'PRICE_FILTER', symbol_info['filters'])[0]['tickSize'])
step_size = float(filter(lambda f: f['filterType'] == 'LOT_SIZE', symbol_info['filters'])[0]['stepSize'])

price = float(filter(lambda x: x['symbol'] == 'ETHUSDT', client.get_all_tickers())[0]['price'])
price = floatPrecision(price, tick_size)
usdt_balance = float(client.get_asset_balance(asset='USDT'))
quantity = floatPrecision(usdt_balance / float(price), step_size)

buy = client.order_limit_buy(symbol='ETHUSDT',quantity=quantity,price=price)
```

While I agree with your thoughts in general, I think we should use both the `connect_timeout` and the `receive_timeout` to appropriately handle common failure scenarios, where the `connect_timeout` protects for:
- slow OS
- overloaded TCP stack
- slow firewall
- inundated network switches

And the `receive_timeout` protects for:
- slow ldap servers
- etc...

I leave it to you, with most visibility into your users needs to determine an appropriate default timeout. I've used the following for our deployment (with a relatively old network, firewall, and ldap server)

```
CONNECT_TIMEOUT = 2
RECEIVE_TIMEOUT = 10
```

On a separate note, I wonder if an overloaded TCP stack that can't clear itself within 3 should actually be a bug, just in a completely different part of the system.

Hello,

Well yes, it is by design since hvad 1.4.0. Even before that, although such models would be shown they emitted an error to the logs. Untranslated models should not exist in a normal hvad setup, because that would make the model incomplete (fields may not exist at all, which is not the same as them having empty/default values).

As you realized while overriding the queryset, fetching the model anyway and emitting errors to the log would make the admin very slow as it forced hvad to either fetch all translations of all models in all languages, or generate an additional query for every fetched model. Just to account for models that should not exist anyway.

FWIW, there was a bug that could lead to the translatable REST support to create untranslated instances when no language and no translatable field was specified on a POST request. It is fixed in the latest release so it creates an empty translation when this happens.

So, I strongly recommend you make sure your apps always create at least one translation for every model. And should you need help to create missing translations, just ask. You should be able to find them using `Model.objects.filter(translations__isnull=True)`

After several days trying to find a solution, I check your flask-sockio and do the following.

My pyramid application uses ```gevent``` and ```socketio``` as a Middleware, this way a request and a socketio connection occurs at different time, so I need to pass application inside middleware.

```
class MySocketioMiddleware(socketio.Middleware):
    """This WSGI middleware simply exposes the application in the WSGI
    environment before executing the request.
    """
    def __init__(self, socketio_app, app, socketio_path='socket.io'):
        self.app = app
        super(MySocketioMiddleware, self).__init__(socketio_app, app, socketio_path)

    def __call__(self, environ, start_response):
        """Create a copy of environ and make app available inside
        """
        environ = environ.copy()
        environ['myapp'] = self.app
        return super(MySocketioMiddleware, self).__call__(environ, start_response)
```
Now inside socket,  access to my app and get the request from environ this way...
```
      app = self.server.environ[sid]['myapp']
      ctx = app.request_context(self.server.environ[sid])
      self.request = ctx.request
```
After this, I can access to request object and session by simple ```request.session```

You can emit to a single client by using the client's session id as the room name:

```python
socketio.emit('an event', {'some': 'data'}, room=sid)
```

The `sid` value for a client is passed in all events. Typically you take it from the connection handler and somehow map it to your user.

Thank you very much for the detailed response. Many of my reasons for creating this package were similar to yours, primarily to break it free from the larger Zipline project to lower the dependency requirements. It sounds like the major point of difference is that I am very willing to include any new contribution from people and let the marketplace decide if the adds are correct or have value (as I don't have the expertise to validate every market). At this point over half the calendars have come from people other than myself (or the original Zipline that this is forked from). It makes sense that you will have higher standards for accepting new material.

All that said I agree that the pydata world should have one source for this data for all the positive benefits you mentioned.

**Here is a possible solution:**
_Givens_:
- This package is just a fork with some modifications from your Zipline it should be certainly possible to keep in sync or merge.
- All the real value is in the calendar definitions more that the utilities around them
- The core "MarketCalendar()" class and the specific implementations for each market is where those definitions are. I have not changed the structure of the MarketCalendar() class in any material way from your original version.

_Plan:_
- I take a look at your new trading_calendars package and see if the changes you made can be brought into this package.
- Once that is done I can let you know the diffs of functionality here that is not in yours.
- Then you (we?) can decide if a merge makes sense given your operating model.
- If a merge does make sense, great we do that.
- If not then perhaps we could refactor out just the modules that define the calendars for each market (like "exchange_calendar_nyse.py") into a new package that we both inherit from. That way each can evolve the wrapper code as needed, but there is one golden source for the actual calendars. Additionally that new "calendars only package" could have a "beta" and "prod" version of the calendars so that the community could add/change calendars in the "beta" stream, my package could import from that, and only when you approve the changes do they get promoted to the prod stream which your package uses.

Let me know what you think.

https://github.com/jacobtolar/redislite-ubuntu-error

redislite installs `redis-server` into `/usr/local/bin/redis-server`; installing the redis distribution installs the binary to the same place, overwriting it.

@dwighthubbard, maybe redislite should either (1) install the redis binaries into a different directory, or (2) rename them with a `redsilite-` prefix, to avoid breaking an existing redis install?

Thank you @cjolivier01 and @piiswrong for your questions. Please find my answers below:

> How many reference count changes occur for a given pointer during alloc? 

That should be 1. Even if copies of the pointers are returned from functions they will be moved instead of copied which should not affect the reference count.

> during free? 

Exactly the number of references. Which in the case of a unique shared_ptr will be 1 as well.

> Is it accessed anywhere during its useful lifetime in such a way that it causes a reference count change? (ie is there an assignment operation where a copy of the shared pointer is taken (thus increasing the reference count) anywhere or is the shared pointer just used from its place in a class/struct data member)?

Potentially NDArray and it's corresponding Chunk could be copied.

> What's the code path/situation where it might have a problem that makes it need to be reference-counted? I there a situation where it might be destroyed while another thread is using it? If so, where?

In my opinion NDArray and it's underlying structure should be Copy-On-Write. In that case you can many cheap copies of the unmodified original that will be just referenced through the shared_ptr. A copy is performed only once you call a mutating method (can be distinguished by the const modifier).

         data_shapes=val.provide_data,
         label_shapes=val.provide_label)

```
@provide_session
def refresh_from_db(self, session=None, lock_for_update=False):
    """
    Refreshes the task instance from the database based on the primary key

    :param lock_for_update: if True, indicates that the database should
        lock the TaskInstance (issuing a FOR UPDATE clause) until the
        session is committed.
    """
    TI = TaskInstance

    print("INSIDE REFRESH dag_id: " + self.dag_id)
    print("INSIDE REFRESH task_id: " + self.task_id)
    print("INSIDE REFRESH exec_date: " + self.execution_date)

    qry = session.query(TI).filter(
        TI.dag_id == self.dag_id,
        TI.task_id == self.task_id,
        TI.execution_date == self.execution_date)

    if lock_for_update:
        ti = qry.with_for_update().first()
    else:
        ti = qry.first()
    if ti:
        self.state = ti.state
        self.start_date = ti.start_date
        self.end_date = ti.end_date
        # Get the raw value of try_number column, don't read through the
        # accessor here otherwise it will be incremeneted by one already.
        self.try_number = ti._try_number
        self.max_tries = ti.max_tries
        self.hostname = ti.hostname
        self.pid = ti.pid
    else:
        self.state = None
```

Having done some reading and performed a bit of a survey of some other (bigish) packages, my recommendation is that we pin to a known, working state in `requirements.txt` for development; and not pin in `setup.py` for distribution; we should probably test against both.

This is based on the following findings:

ipython - not pinned: https://github.com/ipython/ipython/blob/master/setup.py#L200
pytest - not pinned: https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/blob/master/setup.py#L51
pylons - not pinned: https://github.com/Pylons/pyramid/blob/master/setup.py#L40
django - not pinned: https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/setup.py#L50
sympy - not pinned: https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/master/setup.py#L365
requests - setup.py not pinned: https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/master/setup.py#L49
requests - requirements.txt pinned: https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/master/requirements.txt#L1
boto3 - setup.py not pinned: https://github.com/boto/boto3/blob/develop/setup.py
boto3 - requirements.txt not pinned: https://github.com/boto/boto3/blob/develop/requirements.txt

For the second questions: let's imagine you have a complex m3u8 where first segments are unencrypted but, at some point in the time window, encryption starts, so you may have something like this (quite extreme example, time window should never be so big from an encoder):

#EXTM3U
#EXT-X-TARGETDURATION:10
#EXT-X-VERSION:3
#EXT-X-MEDIA-SEQUENCE:0
#EXTINF:10.0,
file0.ts
#EXTINF:10.0,
file1.ts
#EXTINF:10.0,
file2.ts
#EXT-X-KEY:METHOD=AES-128,URI="https://priv.example.com/key.php?r=52",  \
IV=0x9c7db8778570d05c3177c349fd9236aa
#EXTINF:10.0,
file3.ts
#EXTINF:8.0,
file4.ts
#EXT-X-KEY:METHOD=AES-128,URI="https://priv.example.com/key.php?r=53",  \
IV=0xc055ee9f6c1eb7aa50bfab02b0814972
#EXTINF:10.0,
file5.ts
#EXTINF:10.0,
file6.ts
#EXTINF:10.0,
file6.ts


In this example, m3u8.keys() will return 3 keys in the list: None (for the first segments), key1 and key2 (let's call this way for the sake of simplicity).

If you want to get the segments encrypted by last key, you would do:
m3u8.segments.by_key( m3u8.keys[-1] )  

as shown in the readme.rst. The same would apply for the first segments (with key None):

m3u8.segments.by_key( m3u8.keys[0] )

But in this case, this is comparable to 

m3u8.segments.by_key(None)

because there is a 'None' key in the list of keys. Is that clear?

Actually, thinking about it, we could create a KeyList class, with filter methods:
- by_uri
- by_iv
- by_method

This way, it would be possible to make:

  key1 = m3u8.keys.by_uri("https://priv.example.com/key.php?r=53")
  segm = m3u8.segments.by_key(key1)

Maybe something you are interested on implementing yourself? Not difficult at all.

I'm going to fix this for Bleach 2.1, but not for older versions of Bleach. That's going to be a problem for anyone who hasn't updated to Bleach 2, yet.

For those people, I suggest doing something like the following:

```
def remove_invisible_characters(text):
    for c in ('\a', '\b', '\f', '\v'):
        text = text.replace(c, '')
    return text
```

Then wrap `bleach.clean()` calls with that like this:

```
text = remove_invisible_characters(bleach.clean(dirty_text))
```

import rospy
import webrtcvad
import collections
import contextlib
import sys
import time
import signal
import pyaudio
import wave
import scipy.io.wavfile as wav
from deepspeech.model import Model
from std_msgs.msg import String

FORMAT = pyaudio.paInt16
CHANNELS = 1
RATE = 16000
SAMPWIDTH = 2
CHUNK_DURATION_MS = 30  # supports 10, 20 and 30 (ms)
PADDING_DURATION_MS = 1000
CHUNK_SIZE = int(RATE * CHUNK_DURATION_MS / 1000)
CHUNK_BYTES = CHUNK_SIZE * 2
NUM_PADDING_CHUNKS = int(PADDING_DURATION_MS / CHUNK_DURATION_MS)
NUM_WINDOW_CHUNKS = int(240 / CHUNK_DURATION_MS)

vad = webrtcvad.Vad(3) # (0 to 3)  -->  0 is the less aggressive
audiopath = '/home/nvidia/Documents/respeaker.wav' # path to recorded audio file

pa = pyaudio.PyAudio()
stream = pa.open(format=FORMAT,
                           channels=CHANNELS,
                           rate=RATE,
                           input=True,
                           start=False,
                           # input_device_index=2,
                           frames_per_buffer=CHUNK_SIZE)


class Recognizer(object):
    
    def __init__(self):

        rospy.init_node("alfred_listen")
        self.pub = rospy.Publisher('listened', String, queue_size=10)
       
        self.got_a_sentence = False
        self.leave = False

        # for deepspeech_mozilla inferences
        DeepspeechModel=rospy.get_param("~deepspeechModel", "/home/nvidia/Documents/elpimous_utils/output_graph.pb")
        DeepspeechAlphabet=rospy.get_param("~deepspeechAlphabet", "/home/nvidia/DeepSpeech/data/alphabet.txt")
        try :
            self.ds = Model(DeepspeechModel, 26, 9, DeepspeechAlphabet) # model link, cepstrum, context, alphabet.txt
        except: 
            rospy.loginfo('E - missing model params !')

    def write_wavefile(self, data):
        with contextlib.closing(wave.open(audiopath, 'wb')) as audio:
            audio.setframerate(RATE)
            audio.setnchannels(CHANNELS)
            audio.setsampwidth(SAMPWIDTH)
            audio.writeframes(data)

    def inference_result(self):
        fs, audio = wav.read(audiopath)
        decoded = (self.ds.stt(audio, fs))
        print('Inference -> '+str(decoded))
        self.pub.publish(decoded)

    def handle_int(self, sig, chunk):
        self.leave = True
        self.got_a_sentence = True
    
    def vad(self):
        signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, self.handle_int)
        while not self.leave:
            ring_buffer = collections.deque(maxlen=NUM_PADDING_CHUNKS)
            triggered = False
            voiced_frames = []
            ring_buffer_flags = [0] * NUM_WINDOW_CHUNKS
            ring_buffer_index = 0
            buffer_in = ''
    
            print("* recording")
            stream.start_stream()
            while not self.got_a_sentence and not self.leave:
                chunk = stream.read(CHUNK_SIZE)
                active = vad.is_speech(chunk, RATE)
                sys.stdout.write('1' if active else '0')
                ring_buffer_flags[ring_buffer_index] = 1 if active else 0
                ring_buffer_index += 1
                ring_buffer_index %= NUM_WINDOW_CHUNKS
                if not triggered:
                    ring_buffer.append(chunk)
                    num_voiced = sum(ring_buffer_flags)
                    if num_voiced > 0.5 * NUM_WINDOW_CHUNKS:
                        sys.stdout.write('+')
                        triggered = True
                        voiced_frames.extend(ring_buffer)
                        ring_buffer.clear()
                else:
                    voiced_frames.append(chunk)
                    ring_buffer.append(chunk)
                    num_unvoiced = NUM_WINDOW_CHUNKS - sum(ring_buffer_flags)
                    # detect end silence, and stop hearing !
                    if num_unvoiced > 0.9 * NUM_WINDOW_CHUNKS:
                        sys.stdout.write('-')
                        triggered = False
                        self.got_a_sentence = True

                sys.stdout.flush()

            sys.stdout.write('\n')

            data = b''.join(voiced_frames)
            print("finished recording")

            self.write_wavefile(data) # send finished audio data to writing function
            stream.stop_stream()

            self.inference_result() # call the inference function
            #time.sleep(1)

            self.got_a_sentence = False
        
        stream.close()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    try:
       a = Recognizer()
       a.vad()

    except rospy.ROSInterruptException: 
       rospy.loginfo ("Exiting program")
    pass
```

Hi @sabotagebeats, the pagination values you're referring to come from the V3 BC API, whereas this library is for the older V2 API and has not yet been updated for V3.

For V2, best practice is to take the count and your desired page size to determine the total number of pages over which you'll need to paginate to get all objects, and then paginate them. As per your error message, the maximum page size is 250.

To use the V3 API with this library, you can create an OAuthAPIConnection object directly and change the pathing to /v3/ like so:

```
v3client = bigcommerce.connection.OAuthConnection(client_id=client_id,
                                                      store_hash=store_hash,
                                                      access_token=access_token,
                                                      api_path='/stores/{}/v3/{}')
```

This will allow you to use the low-level CRUD interface to interact with the JSON directly, as a workaround for now. An API call would then look like this:

```
products_response = v3client.get('/catalog/products?limit=%s&page=%s'
                                   % (page_size, current_page))
```

And you could access the pagination object in the response like this:

`products_response['meta']['pagination']`

This PR was prompted by my realization that I'm inconsistent with describing Issues and PRs and I don't have a clear structure when submitting them. Sometimes I don't catch all the edge cases when testing (without writing unit tests), and I'm likely not the only one. By adding structure to Issue and PR submissions, we can more explicitly define expectations. I wouldn't think that we'd lose quality submissions due to a little extra work, but of course I'm still relatively new to the game and need structure.

I think what I originally submitted is a bit too much to ask from Luigi-users, but I just wanted to throw something out to get opinions and narrow it down to the necessary.

Regarding **PRs**, when I read them I'm really looking for two things.  
1. What they think they've changed/add and   
2. Why they are doing so / what purpose does this serve.  
Asking them to explain how they tested it is more so just to remind them to write unit tests (if possible) or at least cause them to make sure they aren't going to induce errors (like my last PR). Instead of asking them to write about how they tested their PR, we might just include some text in there to remind them to make sure they've gone through that step on their own.

**Issue** submissions, I feel, just need to inform the reader/reviewer what behavior is expected, what is actually happening, and (ideally) a hypothesis why.

`upstream-status-when-all` is a bit critical after retry-policy per task feature. Assume that, I have multiple etl report to be done at night and some of them have different retry-policy. For example one of them has retry-count as 5, another has it as 2. Luigi is marking parent task as `DISABLED` and shutting down the worker and does not let the task retry itself 5 times because of the other task is disabled after 2 times retry. In the example which I added, this can be seen. I added this config because, I may want to wait for all tasks to retry itself even one is `DISABLED` and settings the task as `UPSTREAM_DISABLED` when all its upstream tasks are `DISABLED` (not includes SUCCESS). This config   is more critical after the per task retry-policy config as I said. But it does not break anything, because its default value is `False` and it works how luigi works currently.

Pycodestyle is the same thing as Pep8, it is simply a newer version. There is some overlap between linters but if you satisfy one you satisfy all.

Pylint, from my experience, is not enough on its own. And its development seemed to have slowed down considerably.

With pylama you can simply switch everything else off you you like - and instead of having to support multiple linters you have one that covers everything besides optional type checking (and there is already mypy for that provided as a plugin).

I recommend you play a bit with pylama and see if you like it. To include pylint you have to install pylama_pylint.

An alternative is to have plugins for each. The main ones:
Pycodestyle (already implemented)
Pydocstyle
PyLint
PyFlakes (already implemented)
McCabe
PyChecker (haven't used so can't comment)
isort (sorts imports, with diff it shows where the imports are not sorted)

This way people can choose exact combo they like. All other I have seen (flake8, prospector etc) are wrappers around different combinations of the above.

Up to palantir to decide!

After some more reflection and analysis I changed my mind. There is a way we can reduce the current 10 minutes generation time to virtually 0: just store the hazard curves/maps in the datastore in exactly the same way the QGIS plugin is expecting them. Currently the storage is different for space sake (it is more compact, not storing the zeros) but then at each call it has be converted. It is best to perform the conversion only once during the calculation runtime and to save a larger datastore file: then the extract/export will become ultra-fast, essentially making caching the outputs on the file system unneeded.

See [this personal branch](https://github.com/ankostis/smmap/tree/leaks_memview) which is a total rewrite based on the conclusions I had layed out in  gitpython-developers/smmap/issues/31#issuecomment-256004958.

> It's worth noting that most objects in python are freed by use of
reference counting, 

Actually all objects were freed on ref-counting, till Python-3.5; since 3.5 that is not the case.
They are freed later, by a GC thread (I guess).
You rarely get to notice that in Linux - you can still delete files that are open.
But you bump into this every time you create a temp-file/folder on Windows, and then try to clean it up, because dead but non-GCed objects still prevent files from deleting.

Now, the "correct" way is to use WeakRef finalizers.
You can see all efforts into these issues by searching for [`tag.leaks`](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/issues?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=label%3Atag.leaks%20).

Any improvement would be gladly accepted.

The problem is real but apparently the `PY_VERSION` variable is only used for documentation purposes so this isn't too severe. Should definitely be fixed or alternative the whole variable should be removed.

The actual bug here is that `interpreter` is the interpreter user gave from the command line and `sys.version` is the version of the interpreter running `run_tests.py`. These two interpreters don't need to be same, and they obviously aren't if we run tests like `python2 run_tests.py --interpreter python3 ...`. This also means that the proposed fix is not correct.

If we want to get the name and version of the interpreter regardless is it given from the command line like `python`, `python2`, `python3.6`, `py -2`, `jython` or `pypy3`, the only way is executing the interpreter with `-V` option and reading the output (need to use `-V` and not `--version` if we care about IronPython that only supports the former). This is exactly what Robot Framework does in it's acceptance test runner. It has a separate `Interpreter` class that has also other related responsibilities. This interpreter name and version detection code could be just copy-pasted to S2L:s runner: https://github.com/robotframework/robotframework/blob/master/atest/interpreter.py#L31

S2L is a global library. That means it's only initialized once, afterwards each suite pulls in the existing instance. In this case, passing in the screenshot_root_directory will only work on your first import of S2L and will be used for the rest of the test execution. If you only want to set it in certain tests you need to explicitly use set_screenshot_directory. Same goes for the run_on_failure argument.

My previous patch for the test (not sim) time stuff meant that when a thread made a call to `time.delay()` that was negative, it would be intercepted in the `FakeTime` class and would wait for the main thread to advance time until its delays were positive again. This meant that threads under test didn't run at a million miles an hour when in the test harness and fail to terminate properly.
The issue here is in the sim harness, when it is running in real time. The call on https://github.com/robotpy/pyfrc/blob/master/lib/pyfrc/sim/sim_time.py#L92 is failing because this is a call from a thread for negative time, which is being sent to the system `time.delay()` and not faked by the test harness.
I think the fix will be to check for negative time in `sim_time.py` - otherwise we get errors with the unit test harness hanging. My team is having problems with this when unit testing a robot with PID loops.
