Thanks for taking the time to respond. :)

----

### TL;DR:

**If the auto-activation of a virtual environment by the Python interpreter doesn't fit with your model of the project, I can proceed with a revision of the documentation patch.**

The remainder of the reply tries to identify where our mental models differ and either gain better understanding of your perspective or try to better communicate my perspective. :)

----

### The start 

> I agree that some clarification in the docs would be useful, I'd prefer different wording.

I had meant to mention that I wasn't attached to the exact wording I suggested, it was more intended as a starting point. :) I think the minimum would be to at least remove the phrase "there's no need for activation".


### Mental models of virtual environments and activation

I'm still somewhat confused about your perspective on the issue but it seems to me to come down to us each having a different mental model about:

 1. how an "isolated virtual environment" is defined and/or implemented; and,
 2. the concept of "activation".

#### What is an isolated virtual environment?

In terms of operation I'd expect a "virtual environment" to *replicate the same experience* as installing the application directly in the global non-virtual environment.

Additionally, I'd expect an "isolated" environment to not be affected by things outside the environment.

I think the key differences in our respective mental models hinge on how we each define what "the same experience" means:

[Okay, on re-reading this, the issue is not about what happens *in* a virtual environment (where we both seem to agree) but about what happens when a Python binary located within a virtual environment is executed directly, so, perhaps assume I wrote that instead. :) ]

 * Your perspective seems to be that--in a global installation--a subprocess Python would use the system Python, thus, in a virtual environment the subprocess Python should *also* use the system Python. 

 * My perspective is that in a global installation a subprocess Python would use the *same* Python as its parent and so in a virtual environment the subprocess Python should also use the *same* Python as its parent.

Thus both perspectives are consistent within themselves but result in different expected outcomes.

#### What is activation?

As I understand it, when an virtual environment is "activated":

 1. The correct Python library search paths for the virtual environment are set (via `sys.path`); and,
 2. The `PATH` (and other environment variables) are modified to ensure the correct Python binary (i.e. the one within the virtual environment) is used.


My initial understanding was that when the `/<virtualenv>/bin/python` binary was executed directly without manually activating the virtualenv that it would "auto-activate" the virtualenv. I now realise that what happens is actually "partial auto-activation" where only the first stage occurs:

 * Key to my confusion around this part is that I don't understand in what scenario(s) is having only a partially activated virtualenv more desirable?

#### My revised mental model

At this point I can make a slight adjustment to the explanation of my mental model of virtual environments and their activation:

 * My expectation is that directly running the Python binary which is located within the virtual environment would either not work at all or "auto-activate" the virtual environment because that means the result would be the same as manually activating the same environment.

But instead, with the current behavior there is a result that is different to *both* a global install and a manually activated virtual environment install because the partially activated virtual environment results in the subprocess using a different Python binary to its parent.

#### Why partial rather than full auto-activation?

If full auto-activation was never expected to be the outcome of running the virtualenv Python binary directly then I wonder why even partially activate it by setting the libraries? And, thus I return to the question of in what scenario is partial activation useful and safe when a full auto-activation isn't?

From your comments in #906 my understanding was that the reason for not implementing full auto-activation was a technical one and you had valid concerns around cross-platform compatibility issues around using wrapper scripts. Thus why I proposed a pure Python solution using standard mechanisms in #1081.

(Side note: I recognize the significance of maintaining backward compatibility; so, if that was desired in this case, the solution in #1081 could require a guard of some sort to ensure existing behavior didn't suddenly change--perhaps the guard could be that the `sitecustomize.py` file enabling full auto-activation is only added when specifically requested by a command line switch.)

### Consideration of the approaches you suggest

[Note: I've swapped the order of the next two quotes.]

#### Use `sys.executable`

> Typically when running a Python subprocess, you should use subprocess.call([sys.executable, ...]) to be sure you're using the same interpreter as your main executable, if that's what you want.

Yes, that's reasonable (and is specifically recommended in [PEP 394](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0394/)) and, although I didn't mention it, one of my earlier attempts to fix the issue was to follow that approach.

*However* in this case the approach of specifying the executable in the subprocess call isn't appropriate because an inventory script doesn't *have* to be a Python script, it can be any form of executable. (As can be seen in the code in question: <https://github.com/ansible/ansible/blob/2cdf31d3a2cc7d15c3362efb2e7f3566e8bdb811/lib/ansible/plugins/inventory/script.py#L169>. Note: that code is not yet released due to a recent reorganisation but the approach used is the same in the current released code.)

#### Don't use `env` shebang

> In my opinion, the Ansible scripts that use #!/usr/bin/env python are wrong, unless the Ansible documentation makes it clear that you need the correct Python interpreter on your PATH
 
There isn't necessarily a direct connection between Ansible and an inventory script (indeed, `ec2.py` was originally designed such that it could just be used directly to get a host list for test purposes) so, as per common usage/PEP-394, to me it doesn't seem unreasonable that a `env` based shebang line is used.

As it happens, the [`ansible` script](https://github.com/ansible/ansible/blob/devel/bin/ansible#L1) itself starts with a `env`-based shebang line but it would appear this gets [intentionally mangled](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/1530702/dont-touch-my-shebang) on installation. (Indeed, if it *didn't* get mangled then AFAICT the virtual environment would have to always be manually activated to work correctly anyway.)

If it were common to run `ansible` (or, indeed, any system installed script) with a Python interpreter that wasn't on the `PATH` I could see it potentially reasonable that Ansible make a note as you suggest. However, given the issue doesn't occur when Ansible is installed globally nor when it's run in a manually activated virtual environment but only in a "partially auto-activated" environment it doesn't seem to me that it's an issue on Ansible's side. (Particularly as the same issue would occur with any other Python-based tool that worked the same way.)

#### Don't use a non-activated virtualenv's interpreter

> (in which case, running via a non-activated virtualenv's interpreter is not following their documentation).

The use of the phrase "non-activated virtualenv's interpreter" suggests that it was never your intention that running such an interpreter would auto-activate the environment? Is there a particular reason why a "partial activation" (i.e. setting `sys.path`) fits your mental model of how virtualenv should work but a "full activation" (i.e. setting the Python interpreter process's environment variables) doesn't?

(The only concern that comes to mind is that somehow a changed `PATH` might interfere with correct operation of some other type of subprocess but if this were the case wouldn't doing the same thing in a manually activated virtual environment also interfere? I guess I'd see it as being more consistent if the virtualenv Python removed its own parent directory from the `PATH` after it was started if this was a concern.)

#### Do update the documentation

> Having said all this, I'd be happy with a comment in the documentation saying that some 3rd party scripts assume that Python is in PATH or that VIRTUAL_ENV is set - if you want to use such scripts without activating your virtualenv, you will need to ensure that you manually set the appropriate environment variables in your code.

Sure...or, they could drop the handy pre-written `sitecustomize.py` into the virtualenv's `site-packages`. :D

But, also, I appreciate you being open to a revised documentation patch. :)

### A summary and proposed next step

In summary, because of my mental model I don't understand why if a virtual environment *can* be auto-activated (in a non-hacky way) by the Python binary that is located within it, what scenario benefits from *not* doing this and only using partial activation?

However, I do understand that you are developing the project to fit the mental model that you have of it and which seems reasonable; so, you're obviously entitled to not implement full auto-activation if you don't see it as consistent with your mental model and having value for the project.

So, if that's still the case, then I can update the documentation patch and will instead suggest the `sitecustomize.py` approach to Homebrew to see if they'll incorporate it.

Thanks again for your time and work on the project!

I was going to comment that I didn't understand why this doesn't work correctly without locking: it would seem the worst that could happen is that you create a connection plus request handler, and then drop them in favour of another, new connection plus request handler. So there would be some wasted work, but not necessarily thread leakage.

But having had another look at `RequestHandler`, I strongly suspect that the issue is (again) with its use of `atexit`: because it runs `atexit.register(self.stop)`, it ensures (via the closure over `self`) that no instance of `RequestHandler` will ever be gc'd until program exit. So the real leak, I would argue, is in `RequestHandler`.

Now if that `atexit` is really unavoidable, then the fix here does of course make sense, but in that case we should make it explicit that `RequestHandler` instances are always non-gc'able. If it can be avoided though, then we should instead fix that and we wouldn't need this one.

Bingo. Just note that though you can go crazy with component parallelism
(the rule is, each addition :p number means that many more Python processes
in parallel doing your work), you shouldn't go overboard with worker
parallelism.

The worker parallelism tells Storm to start up more than one JVM process.
In streamparse topologies, however, the workers are already highly
multi-threaded and the main work they do is serialization/deserialization
of the multi-lang protocol JSON, and forwarding data around the cluster.
So, -p1 or -p2 for beefy machines is typically fine. -p16 or -p32 will be
wasteful. Some people prefer to run with -p2 all the time simply so that if
one dies, another one is around until the dead one restarts.

3. Compile your desired Python version (cf. [here](https://github.com/yyuu/pyenv/wiki/Common-build-problems#error-the-python-ssl-extension-was-not-compiled-missing-the-openssl-lib)):

CFLAGS="-I$(brew --prefix openssl)/include" \
LDFLAGS="-L$(brew --prefix openssl)/lib" \
pyenv install -v 3.5.2

`_multiblock_local_binary_pattern` should be a C function, so define it using `cdef`, that removes the overhead of the Python call when speed is critical .
But `cdef`ed functions can't be called from a Python file, so here is how I'd do it.

In `_texture.pyx`
* Define `cdef _multiblock_local_binary_pattern(....)`.  The pure Cython function
* Define `def multiblock_local_binary_pattern(....)`.  The Python wrapper which does conversion

From *Cython* files you can do
```
from skimage.texture._texture cimport _multiblock_local_binary_pattern
````

Given the history of this issue and #1531, I'm going to close it. I don't think digitize offers a compelling advantage over the existing implementation.

I *will* point out that the current implementation is fragile around edge cases, because of the use of NumPy indexing for mapping. This means that the forward map has size `np.max(labels)`. I could give you the input `np.array([1, 1e10], dtype=int)`, and the resulting forward map would take up 80GB of memory. Therefore, creating a class that does the mapping as fast, and with the same `__getitem__` interface, but using reasonable memory, would be desirable.

>  In my opinion I don't think we use it, or if we ever used it

So far we haven't made much use of the distinction, I think.  Often, knowing the person who signed is enough.  However, the real value of signatures may be revealed in future bug hunting, where people don't know original developers and reviewers anymore.

From this point of view, we are generating signatures to simplify life to *future developers*, so the real value of QA signatures is more longer-term.  Consider future developer D who does not know the original developer O and the original reviewer R and whether R's signature usually meant only light testing, or deep reviewing. > messages.pot

Could be named `messages-py.pot` for better analogy with `messages-js.pot`.  One could then merge the two together for the common pot.

BTW, while we are at it, let's use `invenio.pot` and `invenio.mo` which is the usual naming practice.

> In fact, it may even be (partially) undone before the following release. Would we rewrite git history just for this?

Nope, we do not allow rewriting git commit history in already-pushed branches in the official repository.
But your concern is very valid, the release notes would have the full history there, kind of like:

- Foo: initial release of bar and baz
- Foo: improves baz 
- Foo: improves baz even more
- Foo: drops baz for xyzzy

This can be (1) good for those sites that track `master` (see my *progress report* use case), but it is (2) bad for those sites that track only released versions (see my *release notes* use case).

I don't think it is too bad to report full history.  We plan to have shorter release cycles, so this should not be too probable.  (Plus we should think more *before* making those N changes.)

If we do want to solve this, we could perhaps invent some special markup, e.g. the later commit could say something like:

- INVALIDATES fff111 fff222 fff333

so that script would know not to include release notes from those commits.  Just a possibility.

> We can maintain a structured CHANGELOG file when committing user-concerning or API-affecting changes.

Sound like an old technique. This is how GNU Emacs used to do these things traditionally.  Generally speaking, contributors did not like it too much, as it kinds of duplicates the logging what VCS does. I'd rather generate CHANGELOG from VCS or from tickets than relying on people to update it manually. 
(Since we want to have high level of details there for the *progress report* use case, as in "what incompatible changes does 2.1.0.dev2150413 brings that may break Zenodo?"  Important for sites tracking bleeding edge `master`.)

Though we could naturally combine these techniques, e.g. generate it automatically but allow editing results later.  Like I'm doing with RELEASE-NOTES to clean obvious issues, such as people mistyping module names, making issue to be mis-categorised.

I found a regression in ExtensionClass (python version and also my C version) while working on the C-Extension of Acquisition 

The old C code of ExtensionClass changed an attribute error into the following:
```
class Dummy(Base):
    pass

Dummy().a
AttributeError: a
```

The python version (+ my ported C version) does the following:
```
class Dummy(Base):
    pass

Dummy().a
AttributeError: 'Dummy' object has no attribute 'a'
```

See, the error messages contains the typename of an object.
From my point of view a port should not change behaviour, so I would change the C and python version to hide the typename again.

The python version of Acquisition struggled also with the changed behaviour of ExtensionClass.
A lot of doctests there have asserts for `AttributeError: <name>` (no typename in the error message), but instead of questioning ExtensionClass a fix to Acquisition  was introduced.
https://github.com/zopefoundation/Acquisition/blob/master/src/Acquisition/__init__.py#L745
When ExtensionClass behaves the old way Acquisition could be changed to rely on ExtensionClass to throw the appropriate AttributeError. This would make a port of the C-Extension of Acquisition easier too.


Alternative:
* Keep the regression => Keep the new style Attribute error
* Change all the doctests in Acquisition to assert the new style Attribute error
* Change python version of Acquisition to keep the new style Attribute error

Ok. I've managed to reproduce this locally. There is no issue with zope.component here.

It looks like it's an issue with plone.testing and its layer setup. One of the earlier layers fails with the above mentioned `ConstraintNotSatisfied` error. One of the later layers than runs and encounters a half-setup component registry. It's a persistent component registry, set as current via zope.component.hooks.setSite, without the corresponding db connection ever being committed (as it failed half-way).

To make things more interesting, the `__bases__` of that component registry has two more entries for persistent registries from multiple stacked demo storages.

If I get rid of the broken registry via `zope.component.hooks.setSite(None)` the later code runs through, but probably breaks test layer isolation further.

I think this issue can be closed here, unless someone thinks moving the invariant setup (`self._createLookup()`) into a `__new__` is still worth it.

I've been trying, but I'm afraid I haven't been able to reproduce this anywhere.  All the linux boxes I have access to do just fine on branch #549.  

My best guess is that somehow there is a mismatch between the `_galsim.so` file that is being imported and the libgalsim.so` library that is being installed.  You can check where galsim is being imported from using imp:
```
>>> import imp
>>> imp.find_module('galsim')
(None, '/home/mjarvis/lib/python2.7/site-packages/galsim', ('', '', 5))
```
(Normally, you would just `import galsim`, then look at `galsim.__file__`, but that doesn't work when the import fails.)

You can then check (with ldd) which `libgalsim.so` `_galsim.so` is trying to use at run time.  Make sure that's the one that is the first one found in your LD_LIBRARY_PATH.

These morphologies actually are reconstructed form a stack of up to 6 slices (to get the full axon projection) and I think that, in the notation `S2 "V2 section" -25 25 25`, `S2` is the identifier for this slice (and then it is used in segment identification   `(  -22.38   -23.78    -1.82     0.93 S2)  ; 2` to state that this segment is taken from this slice), the first number of ` -25 25 25` is the Z offset of this slice, and the second and third numbers somehow specifies the thickness (although I don't know the difference between these two numbers; they are always equal in our samples).

The Pneumatik library (which I think uses Neuron for display purpose...?) can load these files without issues... To load them with the current version of NeuroM, a working get-around is to delete 

```
(Sections S1 "V2 section" 0 25 25
 S2 "V2 section" -25 25 25
 S3 "V2 section" 25 25 25
 S4 "V2 section" 50 25 25
 S5 "V2 section" -50 25 25
) ; End of Sections
```

and every subsequent reference to these `SX` identifiers (i.e. replacing  `(  -22.38   -23.78    -1.82     0.93 S2)  ; 2` by  `(  -22.38   -23.78    -1.82     0.93)  ; 2` ).  The original files and the files modified that way are both rendered identically by Pneumatik, meaning that the slice offsets are already applied to the segment coordinates so that the `SX` mentions in these coordinate tuples is probably only useful at this time to be able to track the slice provenance of every segment for subsequent verifications and such... I guess that as a first step, NeuroM could be made compatible with these files using a similar approach (i.e., ignoring the Sections definition header and then ignoring the `SX` mentions in the coordinate tuples )... I'll let you see if you consider that the slice provenance is worth conserving and exposing in NeuroM!

And ... if the API is not threaded, then what happens if it gets a burst of requests to fast to answer?  Do they queue, or do they return an error?  If the latter, we could have problems.

1. If the primary goes down, replicas will start leader race and one of them will promote (automatic failover). Remaining replicas will start streaming from the new primary. 

2. Switchover - this is when you manually trigger switch of the primary to one of the replicas and bring the old primary as a new replica into the cluster. 
You can do a switchover with the help of patronictl: `patronictl switchover <your-cluster-name>`

It could happen that none of the replicas are eligible to become a new primary, because they all are lagging to match behind the old primary. In this case you can trigger a manual failover with the help of patronictl: `patronictl failover <your-cluster-name>`. It fill ask you a few questions and make the new primary from one of the replicas, but you have to specify what replica must be promoted. This is important.

> I can't get this way to work properly - it adds config_file and hba_file entries to postgresql.conf but they get ignored, as those config entries only work as startup parameters to Postgres. Not sure if that was due to the introduction of the dynamic configuration.

Yaeh, before dynamic configuration Patroni was passing all parameters on the command line, what makes it not possible to change values for those parameters without restart. Now only limited amount of parameters is always passed in on the command line:
 - cluster_name
 - hot_standby
 - listen_addresses
 - max_connections
 - max_locks_per_transaction
 - max_prepared_transactions
 - max_replication_slots
 - max_worker_processes
 - port
 - track_commit_timestamp
 - wal_keep_segments
 - wal_level
 - wal_log_hints

Basically these parameters either MUST be the same on the master and replicas or responsible for streaming replication or just needed for Patroni to work, everything else is written into postgresql.conf.

What we can do:
1. In case if the `config_file` is specified and exists (and readable) on the file system we can "include" it instead of `postgresql.base.conf`
2. If the `hba_file` is specified and exists (and readable) on the file system we can pass it on the command line instead of writing into `postgresql.conf`

P.S., `include_dir 'conf.d'` approach will work for you right now without any changes in Patroni, but unfortunately it doesn't cover `pg_hba` case.

In the original #512, we mentioned HATEOAS. It was no accident. It is hard to figure out the proper format and data to send. If you want to have discovery, then it is probably worth implementing [JSON API](http://jsonapi.org/format/#document-links), but I'm not sure just how much it can be useful if the full API is not hypermedia-enabled.
The first propositions read to me as though they would be served with a `application/vnd.api+json` MIME type, but that this endpoint would be the only one to be.

I support the idea of having an “It works!”-style confirmation page rather than a full-blown JSON endpoint.
Reading the user story, a simple text message stating “This is the root of an OpenFisca API. Learn how to use it on openfisca.org/doc/api.` would fit the bill. Using JSON increases consistency and is certainly welcome.
Then again, if you have the time and resources to escalate this to “Let people request the root of API to get a list of the most important endpoints”, that's great! :smiley: This probably needs using standards, though.

There is to me one conceptual point that can make this clearer: do you feel like `/` should be documented as an API endpoint?

1. If yes, then its use cases should be defined, its format should be standard, documented in `/spec`, and should reply with a `200`.
2. If no, then it's just a (welcome!) beacon and it should have a simple phrasing congratulating users for having set up their server and encouraging them to explore the API documentation, and should reply with a [`300`](https://developer.mozilla.org/fr/docs/Web/HTTP/Status/300).

Lacking a larger plan to implement #512, I support option 2.

There are a couple things that could be happening here.
A. There is a clashing jira.py
B. The python jira module is not installed at all

# Problem A
```
➜  ~ python
Python 2.7.15 (default, May 16 2018, 17:50:09) 
[GCC 8.1.1 20180502 (Red Hat 8.1.1-1)] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import jira
>>> jira.__file__
'jira.pyc'
>>> quit()
```
The indicator is that `jira.__file__` will show either `jira.py` or `jira.pyc`

**Solution A**

# Problem B
```
➜  ~ python     
Python 2.7.15 (default, May 16 2018, 17:50:09)

```

The charstring for the glyph for U+8619 is too long according to the [CFF2 CharString Implementation Limits](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/typography/opentype/spec/cff2charstr#appendix-b-cff2-charstring-implementation-limits), which gives the "CharString length" limit as 65,535 bytes and this glyph's charstring length is 71,057 bytes.

However, Sairus Patel pointed out that this is an implementation limit, not a spec. limit per se. The `tx` program is currently treating it as if it were a spec. limit, which we plan to change.

As an aside, @blueshade7 pointed out that there are many integer values in the glyph that were stored as floating point numbers, which is very inefficient. When I converted them all to be stored as integers, the length of the glyph dropped below the 65,535 byte limit.

So, to summarize, the font is not making efficient use of glyph charstring bytes, but it is still a valid font.

I think the question is: what are you trying to do? Why is it relevant to you whether a field is set or not and what do you intend to do with this information?

In proto3, field presence for scalar fields simply doesn't exist. Your mental model for proto3 should be that it's a C++ or Go struct. For integers and strings, there is no such thing as being set or not, it always has a value. For submessages, it's a pointer to the submessage instance which can be `NULL`, that's why you can test presence for it.

Please don't access `msg._fields`. It's a private variable with no defined semantics.

I think we're making a mistake returning a tuple here, and instead should return an iterator.

This would mean that we can treat it as a list (not giving a shit about pagination -- aka, good for scripts that need to go through all topics) as well as a paged-resource if needed.

#### Infiniscroll list

```python
for topic in client.list_topics():
  do_something_with_topic(topic)
```

#### Explicitly paginated

```python
token = request.GET['page_token']
topics = client.list_topics(next_page_token=token)
html_response = template.render(
    {'page_token': topics.next_page_token, 'topics': topics.current_page})
response.write(html_response)
```

The reason I care so much about this is that the first case is incredibly common when people first start using this library... So I want to make the common (intro) case fast.

Compared to today:

#### Infiniscroll list

```python
token = None
while True:
  topics, token = client.list_topics(next_page_token=token):
    for topic in topics:
      do_something_with_topic(topic)
    if token is None:
      break
```

(correct me if there's a better way?)

#### Explicitly paginated

```python
token = request.GET('page_token', None)
topics, token = client.list_topics(next_page_token=token)
html_response = template.render(
    {'page_token': token, 'topics': topics})
response.write(html_response)
```

Note that the paginated version doesn't really change all that much, but the infiniscroll version looks way worse off....

I believe this was answered in the [StackOverflow question](https://stackoverflow.com/a/47842039/101923).

Summarizing here:

> How do we go about refreshing the table_id attribute to make sure we can find the QueryJob that created our targeted view?

The `destination` property refers to where the query results *go*. All queries write to a table: if a destination is not specified at query time, a temporary table is created.

To create a TableReference to your view, create one the same way you do any other TableReference.

```
dataset_ref = client.dataset('my_dataset', project='optional_project_id')
view_table_ref = dataset_ref.table('my_view_id')
```

> Is there a better way of getting the query without having to rely on finding the job that created the view?

Get the table with [`Client.get_table(table_ref)`](https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/latest/bigquery/reference.html#google.cloud.bigquery.client.Client.get_table) and get the original query with the [`Table.view_query` property](https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/latest/bigquery/reference.html#google.cloud.bigquery.table.Table.view_query)

That's how lambdify works. Take something like

```py
import sympy
x = sympy.Symbol('x')
f = lambdify(x, sympy.sin(x), 'numpy')
```

This is (roughly) equivalent to

```py
f = eval("lambda x: sin(x)", globals={'sin': numpy.sin})
```

(the globals dict will also contain tons of other things, but only `sin` is needed). 

In other words, `lambdify` converts a SymPy expression into a lambda function where the globals come from the namespace of the module in question (usually NumPy). 

Note that the globals dict is not exactly `numpy.__dict__`. lambdify adds a [custom translation dictionary](https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/58b661a9aacebbe6f26cdcfbb88dffc722404633/sympy/utilities/lambdify.py#L68), and custom translations can be supplied by the user as well.

afaict, this wouldn't be related to #271 but more likely related to bazel's use of pex.

We're seeing very similar errors with our internal applications built with pex 1.2.13.  The failures are indeterminate though; we have a script that attempts the namespace package import on an unpacked pex directory.  It fails roughly every 30 times.  The only thing that I can think of that's different between the script invocations is the Python process (we're using 3.6), and the only thing that I can think of that's related to this is hash randomization.  On a hunch, we ran the same script with `PYTHONHASHSEED=0` and have yet to see a failure after hundreds of runs of the script.  That somewhat jives with my intuition that it's a `.pth` processing order problem.  I.e. if there's a bad `.pth` file somewhere (or maybe not even bad, but just with an implicit order dependency), and we get unlucky so that pex processes the `.pth` files in the wrong order, the namespace packages can't get imported.  It's a little odd because in 3.6, dictionary iteration order preserves insertion order, so it's not a complete explanation.  There's also a weirdness that if we take a pex built on a local machine rather than our build host, the script never fails.  But maybe the `PYTHONHASHSEED` data point is enough of a clue to help nail down the problem.

Hi!
generally a learning rate of 1.0 is incredibly high and I'm not surprised that the model diverges. I'm not exactly sure how OpenNMT-py normalizes the loss, which is very important to know when trying to compare the two setups. By default Sockeye does not normalize the loss. This means that the magnitude of the loss scales with the number of target words in a batch. For this reason generally we would choose a much smaller learning rate (see the default value of 0.0003). Now when --normalize-loss is turned on, the loss becomes independent of the batch size and one can use a larger learning rate. Effectively a normalized loss with a learning rate multiplied by the number of words in a batch should be equivalent to not normalizing the loss. Note also that from what I know MXNet clips gradients based on the absolute value rather then based on the norm. The norm clipping in PyTorch on the other hand rescales the gradient to make sure the norm is bounded, which in turn could also allow for higher learning rates. 
So long story short because of these implementation details you might need different hyperparameters for the two systems. Specifically a lower learning rate would probably make sense.
OK, that last issue seems to caused by `export LC_ALL=C`. The previous sockeye version was not affected.

@mkolod yes, exactly, if we created fused cells, they are independent for each layer and direction.
I think the reason for creating stacked FusedRNNCells is that MXNet can compute gradients faster, whereas if one would use a single FusedRNNCell for multiple layers, MXNet has can not parallelize gradient computation. But I am not an expert on this.

Right now, we should measure speed & quality on this:
- using the optimized SequenceReverse operator vs. the old version
- additionally adding --fused to use two FusedRNNCells, one for the forward and one for the reverse pass in the BidirectionalEncoder layer.

For multiple encoder layers we should probably also create a SequentialCell with FusedRnnCells in it for the same reason as mentioned above. We might see bigger speedups for such complex models.
One thing that is noteworthy though is that I am not sure FusedRNNCells respect our mixed initialization scheme, where Xavier is used for all weights except .*h2h.* parameters, for which we use mxnet.initializer.Orthogonal.

Here is an up-to-date high-level description of the code:

- It adds two new command-line options enabling pruning and altering the stopping criteria (this latter point isn't as important, and we could remove it)
- It introduces a general ability to inactivate items on the beam. This also allows getting rid of the special treatment for t==1, since this can be handled as a special case of invalidation. In general some of the code at the top of the loop over t is simplified. The pruning code adds some complexity but it is isolated into a single loop that is only used when pruning is enabled.
- Only completed hypotheses are normalized. Pruning is applied in log-prob space against the best-completed hypothesis. By default there is no pruning.

I may not make my question very clear. For preprocessing, I only refer to `normalize-punctuation.perl` and
`remove-non-printing-char.perl`. As the `detokenizer.perl` only reverses the `tokenizer.perl` but not including `normalize-punctuation.perl` and `remove-non-printing-char.perl`, I think it is more consistent to perform tokenization (e.g., v13a) on following tweaked reference:

`cat en-de.de | normalize-punctuation.perl -l de | remove-non-printing-char.perl  > en-de.de.tweaked`

We tried two ways to fix the issue of importing from/exporting to USB, but both failed:

1. `pyfastcopy`
`pyfastcopy` is not increasing the speed for transfer in Python 2 or Python 3. It uses `sendfile()` to transfer files, but unfortunately, its  implementation requires the destination to be a socket (or at least in Mac OS X, it requires that. [The destination could be a file in other systems](https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.html#os.sendfile)). 

2. `subprocess` call to `cp`
This way is actually slower than the original approach we are using. For example, when importing a 1GB channel, the original way takes 47 seconds while `cp` takes 49 seconds.

> @ibnesayeed I don't follow. The hostname:port is how the user currently accesses their local replay system. What is the "listening interface"?

When a service is running on a machine, it can be reached from a network using an IP address and a port number. Basically, the IP address is to locate the host machine while the port number is to locate a process on that host. However, a host machine can have multiple network interfaces that can be listed using `ifconfig` command in Linux machines. A loop back interface is a special network interface that only works on the same machine and does not listen to any traffic from outside. It is accessible from the hostname `localhost` and often mapped to the IP address `127.0.0.1` (with a masking of `255.0.0.0`). It is possible for an application to specify, which of the many network interfaces available on the machine (along with a specific port) should be used to communicate with it. However, if the application wants to listen to all the network interfaces, it can use another special interface available at `0.0.0.0`.

Hostname is just a way to map a name to an IP address, but the application internally not listens on a hostname, instead it listens on an IP address + Port number combination. Ideally, generated URIs should use a customizable hostname (default to `localhost`), but `app.run()` method should use an IP address as host (default to `0.0.0.0`). We need two separate flags to set these two individually and one flag for port number. Without this isolation, it would be difficult to use IPWB behind a reverse proxy or in a container.

@TomasTomecek: That describes the fourth digit from the right:

```c
/* Bits used in the mode field, values in octal.  */
#define TSUID    04000          /* set UID on execution */
#define TSGID    02000          /* set GID on execution */
#define TSVTX    01000          /* reserved */
                                /* file permissions */
#define TUREAD   00400          /* read by owner */
#define TUWRITE  00200          /* write by owner */
#define TUEXEC   00100          /* execute/search by owner */
#define TGREAD   00040          /* read by group */
#define TGWRITE  00020          /* write by group */
#define TGEXEC   00010          /* execute/search by group */
#define TOREAD   00004          /* read by other */
#define TOWRITE  00002          /* write by other */
#define TOEXEC   00001          /* execute/search by other */
```

All those `#define`s would imply that `mode` is four octal digits long, and therefore a null-terminated `char[5]`, with the left-most digit being the set-UID/set-GID/"reserved" value. However, it's actually got a size of 7 (`char mode[8]`). That "0"/"1" discrepancy is in byte 1 (0-indexed), and the user/group/other flags are in bytes 4–6. I would expect the UID/GID/VTX flags to live in byte 3 in this situation, which is definitely a "0" in both cases, so that seems right.

In short, I really don't know what's going on, but I don't think it's as simple as that. The only thing I can think of is that three bytes of the mode are to be ignored for alignment purposes and that perhaps the server is incorrectly comparing the whole 7 characters instead of just the last 4.

Of course, I don't know that this is what's causing the problem at all. It could be the username/group.

I'm trying to wrap my head around whether this issue matches with the problem I'm having. Currently `docker-py` returns fairly painful-to-use responses from the API for a Python application. Even worse if you're writing against multiple versions of the Docker/Swarm API where things may have dramatically changed.

I would prefer being able to parse any version of the API that's supported by `docker-py` to a common format for consumers of `docker-py`. This would make it easier to integrate with a variety of versions of Docker that you may be talking to. Especially useful for a provider of a Docker service.

My current solution is a parser module where you essentially do:

```python
import docker
import docker_parse

client = docker.Client(..., version='auto')
resp = client.info()

friendly_info = docker_parse.parse(client.version(), "info", resp)
```

Thoughts?

fonttools can already recalculate the unicode ranges bit using the "at-least-one" rule, but this is not currently used by ufo2ft:
https://github.com/fonttools/fonttools/blob/dc1a3bd/Lib/fontTools/ttLib/tables/O_S_2f_2.py#L284-L301

As for the codepages, as I said, we do have a script internally that takes the intersection between python's `encodings` tables and a font's Unicode cmap, and calculates "how much" the codepage is covered, and use this to set the respective bits on whenever it is at least 98 %.

We could have ufo2ft automatically do this as a fallback whenever a UFO does not already have user defined values for these bits.
Though I can't make promises as to when I'll be available to work on that, as we are quite busy here at the moment.

Concerning your question about the use of `reconnect_traj_patch()`; this is for the case a dataframe is already linked, and a patch is linked again. Say we have two particles in a linking patch that 'cross over', and they were linked as follows initially:
```
   frame 3    a   b
   frame 4     ab
   frame 5    a   b
```
The `relink` routine starts at frame 3 with the already existing labels, but arrives at a different outcome:
```
   frame 3    a   b
   frame 4     ba
   frame 5    b   a
```

In that case, the function `reconnect_traj_patch` is necessary to adapt the labels (`a -> b`, `b -> a`) in the remainder of the dataframe.

There are also some tests that check this, I will have a look whether this crossover example is in there.

Well, we've done some early investigation into scraping PDFs, but I don't have enough time to keep working on this atm, so here are my notes:

1. The first approach I tried was to use [`pdfquery`][pdfq]. The big problem I ran into there was that it doesn't seem to preserve links inside PDFs. I [filed an issue about this][issue], so perhaps a solution will be found. If none is found, `pdfquery` is a dead end.

2. @brianwc did an experiment with using pdftohtml to convert the pdf to html that we can then scrape. I think this is a good way forward and that the likely solution here is to override Juriscraper's `_parse` or `_download` method to:

 1. Download the PDF that contains the index of cases.
 1. Convert it to HTML using `pdftohtml`.
 1. Put that HTML in memory and then hand it over to the scraper as the source.

 I think this strategy will actually work quite well, but I it's an area that'll need more research, since we haven't done it before. I'd welcome anybody to try to sort this out.

For now, I have to dedicate my efforts elsewhere and Alamaba is *OFFLINE*. Please help if you can.

[pdfq]: https://github.com/jcushman/pdfquery
[issue]: https://github.com/jcushman/pdfquery/issues/28

I've been watching this repo for a little while.  It feels likes a side project at the moment, particularly because of the `print` statements.  But, also because it seems a little Java-y.

> Python 3 Support

Adding to your points:

- Python 3.2 is a bad test target.  It was the version where some over confident changes were introduced, like not recognizing unicode literals.
- I think supporting Python 2.6 is the biggest issue as far as version support is concerned.  I don't think existing customers on old distros will be jumping to use a different library if their setup has been working for this long.  Additionally, it could cause development to be slower since there's significant differences between 2.6 and 2.7.  Aside from some module rearrangements and unicode strings, 2.7 and 3.3+ are mostly source compatible if newer language features are avoided.
- Supporting 3.3 should still be a goal.  Python's support schedule isn't the same as distro support schedules.
- The source should be actively developed for 3.4+, then fixing the lower versions when tests fail for them.

> Are there any plans to introduce support for the JSON API?

I think it would be nice if there was a RESTful API.  It would remove the need to maintain several SDKs for the same service.  XML transport is the absolute worst for a public API if the goal is to onboard developers quickly.

@dancosta We've released versions of `nb_conda`, `nb_conda_kernels` and `nb_anacondacloud` through the official Anaconda conda repos that should resolve the issues you are seeing! @michaelpacer sorry for the delay!

```shell
conda update nb_conda nb_conda_kernels nb_anacondacloud
```

As-installed kernel names that are eligible for being `default` (e.g. `python2`, `python3`, `ir`, etc.) will be maintained unmodified, so they should always appear there.

The downside is as many as three options for the current env, i.e. if you are in root, you will see _Python 2_ and _Python [root]_ and _Python [default]_, you can now reproducibly capture which environment your kernel should run against.

To my mind, conda environment features are a little broken concept. 

I guess the original intention on providing them was good: to ensure all the packages in an environment to work nicely together. However, because they are a property of the environment, and their priority on resolution is so high, they give the solver a hard time and hamper package isolation. It is not rare to see cascaded changes happening because of unintended package inter-dependencies - that I want to install a version of opencv that is built against openblas should not mean all my stack needs to be updated to use openblas.

I personally would like to see conda features to be deprecated in favor of more standard ways of establishing relationships between packages. A good model would be to have predicates like "provides" (like in "tensorflow-gpu provides tensorflow") and "conflicts" (like in "tensorflow-gpu conflicts tensorflow"). 
At least make "features" something that "a package provides", not something that an "environments asks for". This would make easier for package maintainers to build different variants of a package and for users, which are usually grown-ups, to select the version of each package they want, without the jugglery and sweating that sometimes we need to endure.
Please use our packages available via conda.  We do not support arbitrary efforts to compile from source.

Isn't this related to our metadata hotfix discussion? We have two options: 1) fix the external copy of the metadata, and leave the file the same, or 2) rebuild the package, changing the MD5. There is no third option to bump the build number: when metadata is broken, it must be fixed.

It seems to me, however, that assuming the identical filenames means identical MD5s is problematic. We don't make that assumption within conda (or when we do, it's a bug).

So, we've been doing some experimentation with using ACLs on a group directory and they are not working. Somehow, when conda installs things, it is able to apply permissions that are overriding or circumventing the ACLs.

We created a sandbox directory `permstest`. 
```
balter@exalab3:.../permstest$ getfacl .
# file: .
# owner: burchard
# group: BioCoders
# flags: -st
user::rwx
group::rwx
other::---
default:user::rwx
default:group::rwx
default:group:BioCoders:rwx
default:mask::rwx
default:other::---
balter@exalab3:.../permstest$ getfacl miniconda3
# file: miniconda3
# owner: balter
# group: BioCoders
# flags: -s-
user::rwx
group::rwx
group:BioCoders:rwx
mask::rwx
other::---
default:user::rwx
default:group::rwx
default:group:BioCoders:rwx
default:mask::rwx
default:other::---
```
When I or anyone else creates files or directories in that directory, everything "works":

```
balter@exalab3:.../permstest$ echo "main (){}" > test.c
balter@exalab3:.../permstest$ gcc test.c
balter@exalab3:.../permstest$ touch touchtest
balter@exalab3:.../permstest$ mkdir dirtest
balter@exalab3:.../permstest$ ./miniconda3/bin/python -c "f=open('filecreatedbypy','w'); f.write('test'); f.close()"
balter@exalab3:.../permstest$ ./miniconda3/bin/python -c "import os; os.mkdir('dirbypy')"
balter@exalab3:.../permstest$ ls -lrt
total 19875
-rw-rw----+  1 balter   BioCoders 33905474 Feb  2 23:05 Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh
drwxrws---+ 12 balter   BioCoders     4096 Feb  3 09:05 miniconda3
-rw-rw----+  1 balter   BioCoders       10 Feb  3 12:07 test.c
-rwxrwx--x+  1 balter   BioCoders     6262 Feb  3 12:08 a.out
-rw-rw----+  1 balter   BioCoders        0 Feb  3 12:09 touchtest
drwxrws---+  2 balter   BioCoders     4096 Feb  3 12:09 dirtest
-rw-rw----+  1 balter   BioCoders        4 Feb  3 12:09 filecreatedbypy
drwxrws---+  2 balter   BioCoders     4096 Feb  3 12:09 dirbypy
```

When I installed miniconda, the permissions went in as 770 as hoped for. However, when I or anyone else installs, they are going in as 755. This means that only the installer will be able to remove, update, etc.

Conda is simply a python program, and it invokes other compilers and installers. So based on the experiments above, it seems like anything done with a python program or system compiler should result in the expected permissions.

But, clearly conda is able to do something we don't understand regarding how it is able to override permissions.

Any clarity that can be shed on this would be greatly appreciated!

Hi, I have been having the same problem and I believe I have found the source of the issue. I did some digging in `conda/fetch.py` and found that it appears the function `get_proxy_username_and_pass` (which prompts the user) is being called multiple times at the same time. I then found this line (245):

    with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(10) as executor: 

I changed the 10 to a 1 and everything worked!

My guess is that a bunch of threads are trying to fetch the package index, and are all failing and prompting the user for proxy details at the same time.
The PR associated with 92ff4c8 is https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/2374.

This should be fixed with https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/1727.  It adds and removes paths as a block of strings from the front of PATH.  It only removes the first set of such a block.

Please review and let me know if it does not fix the issue.

@FrancescAlted With regular `range` you of course loose the ability to process your chunks in parallel.

I think @CartVaartjes was asking for a silber-bullet: parallel processing of chunks, in-order results, without wait... Intuitively this sounds like a classic application for the producer-consumer pattern but unfortunately with openmp (cython threads) that is not a pattern that lends itself to easy implementation.

@CarstVaartjes You might remember I also had this question when I started my multiprocessing branch of `bquery`.

When you use `i in prange(0,100)` with two threads, thread 1 gets the range (0:50) while thread 2 gets the range (51:100). Within each thread, the results are in-order but since they are processed in parallel you end up with your results in a mangled mess.

There are several ways of dealing with this:

 1. Since you have the variable `i` you know what chunk your result belongs to, so you can save the result out-of-order. (I.e. using the save equivalent of `_getitem()`.) This is what I did initially with multiprocessing `bquery`. - Not too elegant, since the natural chunksize of your result array might be quite different from that of the source array.

 2. You could save the results into a results array in the order they arrive but remember the order in which they were saved. Then reorder in a second step. This deals with the chunksize-issue and is easy to implement but always sounded like "cheating" to me...

 3. You could buffer the results from each thread in a thread-local buffer with a buffer size equal to the optimal chunksize of the results array and write these buffers as properly numbered out-of-order results array chunks to disk. This also gets around the mis-adjusted chunksize-issue and the wait-issues but is a bit of a programming/debugging challenge.

 4. You could go the easy road and play with the `schedule=dynamic` and `chunksize` parameters of `prange`. Depending on the structure of the data you are processing, this can help alleviate the issue of the wait mechanism with slow chunks by assigning "super-chunks" (consisting of multiple chunks) to the threads. If you only wait for each super-chunk, the difference in processing time of an individual chunk may (or may not) average out over the super-chunk. - Not really a good all-purposes solution but maybe useful in some particular cases.

I have a semi-working implementation of the thread-local buffer approach in an unpublished multiprocessing `bquery` branch. Let me know if you are interested in using it as a quarry for code segments.

Of course in all cases of bcolz chunk processing, even a sub-optimal multiprocessing solution is likely to be faster than a single-core solution. Its just the scalability with the number of cores that suffers, right?

I find out a workaround for this issue.  Just install the latest `az`-dev version solved my problem.  Here is the steps to workaround this:

1. [Install Python](https://chocolatey.org/packages/python)
  `choco install python -y`
2. [Install pip](https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/installing/)
  `wget https://bootstrap.pypa.io/get-pip.py`
  `python get-pip.py`
3. [Install the latest Azure CLI 2.0](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/install-azure-cli?view=azure-cli-latest) using **pip**
  `pip install --pre azure-cli --extra-index-url https://azurecliprod.blob.core.windows.net/edge`

For all of our create and update commands, we output what the service responds with. `set` is just the verb we use when we don't have separate create and update commands (because it's a better command name than "create-or-update").  So outputting what the service returns is completely consistent with the rest of the CLI.

The issue is that app settings are pretty much just generic key-value pairs. We could:
1. have the command return nothing (this would be inconsistent with other set/update commands) 
2. suppress all displaying of appsettings for this command (necessitates a show call if you want to see the appsettings)
3. apply a heuristic to try and guess whether a setting is sensitive or not (easy for things to fall through the cracks)

If you used the show command, these values would be displayed as well.  If that is acceptable, then I don't see why we wouldn't just lease the behavior as-is.

The default Python on Webapp is considered deprecated, since it has not been updated for a long time. The "pip" installed is old, and it's why you can't install "msrest".

So the tutorial will work for basic dependencies, but will likely fail for something more subtle (like the SDK for Python, numpy, etc.)

Short term solutions (ordered)
- Use Linux preview and containers:
https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/app-service/containers/tutorial-custom-docker-image
https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/app-service/containers/tutorial-docker-python-postgresql-app
https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/updates/new-python-solutions-for-web-app-for-containers/
- Use an extension of Python: 
https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/visualstudio/python/managing-python-on-azure-app-service
https://github.com/Azure-Samples/app-service-msi-keyvault-python
- Update pip in a venv using Kudu
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/43970307/azure-functions-installing-python-modules-and-extensions-on-consumption-plan/43984890#43984890

>> The solution you mentioned that can query all rows in one call (even if there are more than 1000 rows) is not supported in our Python library at this time. There may be support for that using our .Net WCF client which leverages the OData library directly.

There is no way to query more than 1000 rows in one HTTP call. This is a service level choice.  AsTableServiceQuery processes the continuation tokens for you and may do many service calls. Given network time it's not surprising that querying the service is slower than excel for range queries. Like JC mentioned, take a look at the performance checklist, specifically at https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/documentation/articles/storage-performance-checklist/#subheading30.

The basics of a pre-receive hook are reading some refs from stdin and making decisions based on them.

These decisions _may_ involve checking out a revision and running `pre-commit` cli against them.

You may find the following invocations of pre-commit useful for implementing this:

```
# Run against a set of files
pre-commit run --files file[, file[, file ...]]
# All files
pre-commit run --all-files
# used by pre-push, but could also be used for pre-receive
pre-commit run --origin ref --source destref
```

Here's a sample one that some of our repositories use internally, it's a little rough around the edges so there's probably bugs:

```bash
#!/bin/bash

set -e

repodir=$GL_REPO_BASE/$GL_REPO
venvtmp=$repodir/pre-commit-venv

export -n GIT_DIR

virtualenv $venvtmp
echo pre-commit==0.7.6 > $venvtmp/requirements.txt
$venvtmp/bin/pip install --requirement $venvtmp/requirements.txt  2>/dev/null >/dev/null

repo=`pwd`

z40=0000000000000000000000000000000000000000
while read oldsha newsha refname
do
    # Skip ref deletions
    if [ "$newsha" = $z40 ]
    then
        continue
    fi

    tmp=$(mktemp -d --tmpdir=/tmp)
    trap "rm -rf $tmp" 0
    (
        cd $tmp

        # Create the repo, but use alternates to avoid cloning
        git init
        git remote add origin $repo
        echo $repo/objects > .git/objects/info/alternates
        git config advice.detachedHead false
        git checkout $newsha --

        if [ "$oldsha" = $z40 ]
        then
            # New branch requires all files checked
            args="run --all-files"
        else
            # Only run on changed files
            args="run --origin $newsha --source $oldsha"
        fi
        $venvtmp/bin/pre-commit $args
    )
```

Let's take C API. Examples are little pieces of code that use the API of the library to show how to use it or how great and simple it is. Current C++ examples in Bullet:

* Not simple,
* Don't use C API,
* Interconnected,
* In fact, packaged into the library.

They were examples of C++ API, but not from the point of view of C API.

By definition, if you take VR project seriously, it is no longer an example, not little piece of code to show API usage.

I'm for taking C API seriously too, that's the point. First step is to define its scope: physics library should do physics and do it well. Not bundle GUI for example. Very little one can do with GUI from C API or Python, but it's there! 

And please don't get me wrong, I think Bullet is a great project and I'm trying to help: let's limit the scope of C API, make it work within this scope, not implement big separate software projects inside the library but rather gradually simplify and disentangle the code.

I agree that defining the methods more explicitly in production code and getting rid of the dynamic `setattr` would be a good idea. Additionally, having everything explicitly listed in tracing.py, while lifecycle.py is all implicit, is unsatisfactory. I already said that
> if you don't like this dynamic generation, the methods could also be "precompiled" statically, but I didn't want to make all that writing effort right now.

So I agree with points 3 and 4 of your list, but I'd strongly suggest keeping 1 and 2:

1. The `_tm` function was just for me being too lazy to write `{"start": start, "end": end}` all the time and wanting to catch typos. So imagine `uber_timeout = attr.ib(type=float, default=None, metadata={"start": "request_start", "end": "request_end"})` standing there. This brings the definition of the possible timeout values and the lifecycle states they span very closely together, making this logic also very easy to understand, document, maintain and validate, being visually similar to the [tabular representation](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/issues/2768#issuecomment-373142177). If this would be "precompiled" into the code of each respective `send_*` function, the link between timeouts and their respective lifecycle events would be a lot harder to see, let alone maintain.
2. The `SIGNAL`s list allows some introspection upon the various available signals, allowing generic code to work with them and preventing unnecessary duplication and redundancy, which are another major source of errors. Image making a change to the `send_*` functions, but forgetting one of them. As the duplicated code from `sender` in each of them is going to be very long, this problem might also be very hard to spot.

I'm still in favour of defining each of the send methods explicitly, but here's how I'd do what without getting too much redundant, duplicated code: using collectors and dispatchers.
All `RequestLifecycle.send_*` methods would be explicitly defined: 
```python
async def send_request_start(self, method, url, headers):
    return await self._send_signal(Signal.REQUEST_START, TraceRequestStartParams(method, url, headers))

async def send_request_end(...
```
Here, a normal member method `_send_signal` would take the place of the respective `sender` instances. It would _collect_ all the different send calls, their signal and their parameters in a generic way. `Signal` could be made an enum, still allowing introspection and generic code, without the danger of typos in stringly-typed code.
```python
async def sender(self, signal, params):
    # record timestamp
    self._signal_timestamps[signal] = time.time()

    # cancel all running timeouts that end with this signal
    while self._set_timeouts[signal]:
        timeout_handle = self._set_timeouts[signal].pop()
        timeout_handle.cancel()

    # send on_signal to all trace listeners
    await asyncio.gather(
        trace_config.dispatch_signal(signal, self._session, trace_context, params)
        for trace_config, trace_context in self._trace_configs
    )

    # start all timeouts that begin with this signal and register their handles for the end signal
    for end, timeout in self._set_timeouts[signal]:
        assert isinstance(self.request_timer_context, TimerContext)
        at = ceil(self._loop.time() + timeout)
        handle = self._loop.call_at(at, self.request_timer_context.timeout)
        self._set_timeouts[end].append(handle)
```
Notice how only the parameters and the call to `trace_config` (now without `getattr`) changed from the intial `sender`. As we dropped the `Trace` class, the signal would go to `TraceConfig` directly. After receiving a generic signal, the `TraceConfig` could then _dispatch_ it to its individual handlers:
```python
class TraceConfig:
    def __init__(self, trace_config_ctx_factory=SimpleNamespace):
        self._on_request_start = Signal(self)
        self._on_request_chunk_sent = Signal(self)
        ...

    async def dispatch_signal(self, signal, session, trace_context, params):
    	if signal == Signal.REQUEST_START:
    		self.on_request_start.send(session, trace_context, params)
    	elif signal == Signal.REQUEST_END:
    		self.on_request_end.send(session, trace_context, params)
    	elif signal == ...

    @property
    def on_request_start(self):
        return self._on_request_start

    @property
    def on_request_chunk_sent(...
```
If you don't like the long `elif` chain, one could also use a shorter dict, depending on how dynamic you like it.

With this approach, all public important functions would still be explicitly defined, but the a little more complicated glue code between them would not be duplicated.

At the same time, there is an elephant in the room: our JavaScript APIs are
not stable, have never been stable, and we are about to break all of them
completely. After that, they will be stable in the versions/semver sense.

Here is my preference: because our JS APIs are not public, we are free to
break them in each of the 4.x releases. It is my understanding that in
semver, internal refactoring is fine across point releases.

The alternative is to do refactoring in a branch until 5.0, which I don't
feel is reasonable if master evolves quickly at the same time. I don't
think we would ever be able to merge the two.

Mergeopt basically allow to merge the configuration with the default of the the parent class, so that if you set the option on `Cell`, it affects `MarkdownCell`, `CodeCell`, `RawCell`, but if you **also** set the config on `MarkdownCell` the config in MarkdownCell override the value set in `Cell`, the the option dict bubble down to `Cell`, which  set an attribute on the instance which is the use.

This is highly historical behavior has we had many more cell types before, like HtmlCell and header 1-6. 
Such an abstraction layer might not be needed anymore.

I vote for #1. I think all automated scripts will be error prone and maintenance heavy as we will not be able to foresee all issues and changes in policies. Since maintainers will do the merge, we can rely on their judgment to decide which digit to increase. This can even help with optimistic merge since in doubt only the last digit could be updated. We can use 6 digits at any point to identify unstable versions if the release process is fixed accordingly. That would help to move things along. Issues can stay open to note what still remains to be fixed and we can merge any PR that has been stalled for more than a week and allow other contributors to make another PR to finalize the work instead of being stuck in the waiting game. Of course, having a dev branch or dev repos would resolve this issue as well and allow everyone to contribute and keep the release branch clean and stable with only PRs that have been vetted by the maintainers. Of course this meens that there might be some PRs with more than one issue fixed or the dev branch becoming filled with unmerged fixes and features (I am being overly optimistic here ;-) .

the registry object still exists: https://github.com/OCA/OCB/blob/10.0/odoo/modules/registry.py#L23 and probably won't go away any time soon

But passing environments where we would pass registries now would be a quite welcome change. We can't do this everywhere, because the Environment context is only created quite late compared to the registry.

Looking through the code, I only count four instances of general functions that take a registry at all (https://github.com/OCA/openupgradelib/blob/master/openupgradelib/openupgrade.py#L477, https://github.com/OCA/openupgradelib/blob/master/openupgradelib/openupgrade.py#L1098, https://github.com/OCA/openupgradelib/blob/master/openupgradelib/openupgrade.py#L587, https://github.com/OCA/openupgradelib/blob/master/openupgradelib/openupgrade.py#L539). For those few, I think we can easily make them autodetect what we got. For the version specific ones, we simply can decide that from 9.0 on, a standard openupgradelib signature should be `(env, ....)` and we're done.

Note that we did already decide that the migrate decorator will pass an env starting from 10: https://github.com/OCA/openupgradelib/blob/master/openupgradelib/openupgrade.py#L1043-L1046

I think of Jacobian-vector-product(fun) as doing the same computation as
fun, with an additional computation of jvps going on 'above' at the same
time. The jvp computation has the same memory complexity as fun itself, so
for any function the peak memory usage of Jacobian-vector-product(fun) will
be 2x that of just running fun.

Apply this argument to Jacobian-vector-product(grad(fun)) and you see that
the peak memory usage must be 2x that of grad(fun).

On further investigation it appears this issue is caused due to the way the `args` are built up for the `cli_register` function in `blockstack_client/actions.py`

```
def cli_register(args, config_path=CONFIG_PATH, force_data=False,
                 cost_satoshis=None, interactive=True, password=None, proxy=None,
                 make_profile = None):
    """
    command: register
    help: Register a blockchain ID
    arg: name (str) 'The blockchain ID to register'
    opt: zonefile (str) 'The path to the zone file for this name'
    opt: recipient (str) 'The recipient address, if not this wallet'
    opt: min_confs (int) 'The minimum number of confirmations on the initial preorder'
    opt: unsafe_reg (str) 'Should we aggressively register the name (ie, use low min confs)'
    """
```
`args` is made up of the following values:-
```
Namespace(action='register', min_confs=None, name='jabbslad.id', recipient=None, unsafe_reg=None, zonefile=None)
```
From a cursory glance it looks like the value of `args` is automatically set by parsing the string literal doc underneath the method declaration and pulling out all the args. 

I think this will mean the following variables will never get set correctly using `getattr` because the attributes will always exist with a value of `None`:-

```
L1277: min_payment_confs = getattr(args, 'min_confs', TX_MIN_CONFIRMATIONS)
L1278: unsafe_reg = getattr(args, 'unsafe_reg', 'False')
```

@csuwildcat I've read the DID/DDO spec and I noted the following:

The document seems to only indirectly take a position on the storage of keys, differentiating between signature and encryption keys, or which cryptographic algorithms to use for signing or encryption.

Section 5.12. Signature (Required) states that the DDO is required to be signed with a valid JSON-LD signature and that the DDO must have a signature block like:

```
{
    "signature": {
        "type": "LinkedDataSignature2015",
        "created": "2016-02-08T16:02:20Z",
        "creator": "did:76d0cdb7-9c75-4be5-8e5a-e2d7a35ce907/keys/1",
        "signatureValue": ""
    }
}
```
So this implies the use of JSON-LD signatures. The latest document (that I am aware of) that describes linked data signatures is:

https://w3c-dvcg.github.io/ld-signatures/#signature-suites

Which takes pains to describe the metadata for a signature, but does not specify a signature suite (other than using RSA as the example).

I looked for actual implementations of JSON-LD signatures (something we are interested in using for Chainpoint proofs) and that implementation, in Javascript, can use `RSA` or `EcdsaKoblitzSignature2016` keypairs. `EcdsaKoblitzSignature2016` was only added last month and they previously called it `BitcoinSignature2016`. They appear to be using the [CoMakery/bitcore-message](https://github.com/CoMakery/bitcore-message) implementation of that signature algorithm to test against.

https://github.com/digitalbazaar/jsonld-signatures

Appendix Section A.2 (Verification Keys) provides the start of an example related to Sovrin which uses `ed25519` signing keys. They do Base58 encoding (instead of the Base64 I showed in my example above).

So the bottom line is that I don't see, at least in the docs I have reviewed, any strong opinions on:

* signing or encryption key algorithms
* how to encode keys
* how to identify keys
* how to make keys available (e.g. a specific property name)

Crypto is hard.

To me this is simple:
- `warn_on_args` causes deprecation warnings to emit at the hook **call** line as well as the `hookimpl` def
- old hook calls continue to work just fine but now with the warnings for any call or `hookimpl` that uses the deprecated args
- eventually hook call sites change when the arg is fully removed or else you'll get the [classic `pluggy` error](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pluggy/blob/master/pluggy/manager.py#L223) 
- we'll need a slight bit of extra logic [to **warn differently** on deprecated args (i.e. those listed in `warn_on_args`) which are in the spec but not in the impl](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pluggy/blob/master/pluggy/hooks.py#L279)

Implementing this will give good reason to finally bring in #57 as well since we'll want to keep track of which args per `hookspec` are *deprecated*.

@pytest.hookimpl(hookwrapper=True)

    upload_generated_html_file_to_somewhere(session)

To have the automation without relaying on setup_requires there could be some code in the setup.py that would try to import pip and use it to install as much of those packages as possible before passing remaining list as setup.

Example of setup.py
```python
from setuptools import setup
import traceback

extra_params = {}
setup_requires = ['setuptools_scm']
try:
    import pip
    pip.main(['install'] + setup_requires)
    setup_requires = []
except Exception:
    # Going to use easy_install for
    traceback.print_exc()

setup(
    # [... other parameters here ...]
    setup_requires=setup_requires
    # [... other parameters here ...]
)
```

Using Pip instead of easy_install has a lot of advantages and having to deal with one installation tool instead of two would be nice and make CI behaviour more deterministic.

To recap what's going on here (as a tl;dr and to clear my thoughts): 

  * We want to use reload instead of restart in both Apache and Nginx, but:
     * Reloading configuration (and it coming to an effect) takes undefined time, depending on the amount of configuration present etc.
     * There's an underlying keepalive issue with Apache child processes.
     * Polling via HTTP may not be feasible in some corner configuration cases.
     * Apache documentation states that there may be (is?) some corner cases where the new configuration will not be reloaded even though the return codes state so.
  * We can use apache2ctl -S to determine some parts of active vhost configuration, but it doesn't take the old child processes running and old configuration in to account.
  * Suggestion about letting the user decide if they want to use reload instead of restart, but let them know about the possible shortcomings.

@menudoproblema Hm.. Yeah, I guess that may be a problem. Honestly, upstream in `dateutil`, I'm deeply wary of any extensions to the RFC. For just excluding specific dates from certain series, I'd probably just use `heapq.merge` with multiple `rrule`s, and use `filter` or a generator expression on the ones you want an exclusion for.

That said, such a thing wouldn't give you the `.after` and `.before`  behavior you'd want. Probably it's worth looking at the RFC and its relevant extensions to see if it specifies anything.

Duplicate of #348. This is a somewhat complicated question, since it's not actually possible to do this in a general way. I'm also not enthused, in general, about the idea of having time zones in `rrule`, since it seems ill-defined and may lead to bugs (since `rrule` is basically doing "wall time" manipulations and doesn't really handle ambiguous times well). Some consideration will need to be made as to how best to handle this, with reference to the RFC.

I think the best way to handle it is to use naive datetimes for the `rrule` and store the appropriate time zone separately, to be applied to the **results** of the `rrule`, not the inputs.

I don't plan on adding a whitelist feature because it would be redundant and potentially confusing API. I think the OP's use case can be solved with a base class that uses the `on_bind_field` hook. Something like

```python
from marshmallow import Schema, fields

class ReadOnlySchema(Schema):
    """Schema that every field dump-only if it doesn't explicitly set load_only=True.
    """
    def on_bind_field(self, field_name, field_obj):
        # Any field that is not load-only will be set as dump-only
        if not field_obj.load_only:
            field_obj.dump_only = True


class MySchema(ReadOnlySchema):
    id = fields.Str()
    password = fields.Str(load_only=True)

schema = MySchema()

assert schema.fields['id'].dump_only is True
assert schema.fields['password'].load_only is True
assert schema.load({'id': 'ignored', 'password': 'secret'}).data == {'password': 'secret'}
assert schema.dump({'id': 'abc12', 'password': 'ignored'}).data == {'id': 'abc12'}
```

As for the names `dump_only` and `load_only`--these make sense in the context of the `dump_only` and `load_only` *field constructor* parameters. @maximkulkin does have a point that the `class Meta` options are used primarily by libraries that dynamically generate fields and therefore should be implemented in those libraries. That said, I think we'll keep those for the time being, if only to maintain compatibility.

Also, I generally try to avoid parameter names that have a negative word in them, e.g. "skip", because double negatives (`skip_load=False`) add cognitive load for the user.

Closing for now. We can reopen if further discussion is necessary.

since 2.7 there is a `flushInput/reset_input_buffer` call at the end of `open()` maybe that makes a difference.

When a "dense" data stream is received (no pause between bytes) and the receiver is reset, it may lock onto a random 0 in the stream instead of a real START bit, which makes it receive "garbage". It will reacquire synchronization after 10 bits (depending on settings) idle line.

It may be that `reset_input_buffer` is passed down to the hardware (with some drivers) and also resets the receiver, not only the buffered bytes, triggering the conditions described above.

you could
1) try if commending out `reset_input_buffer` to see if it makes a difference
2) implement some synchronization that throws away partial packets / garbage until a correct set of data is received.

> Am I correct that the adjust parameter only appears in the CEC's PV performance model in "the" short-circuit temperature coefficient, even although it might itself be calibrated using other "measured output values" such as various datasheet temperature coefficients?

Yes, Adjust is a calibration parameter in the CEC performance model, appearing in both the equation for the short circuit current temperature coefficient and the equation for the open circuit voltage temperature coefficient betaVoc.  However, betaVoc is a model output and is not used in the calculation of the IV curve.

> Furthermore, when computing, say, a Voc value at a temperature different from reference conditions, can one expect that using an datasheet (or adjusted?)
