I couldn't pick up the glitch at 100ms but setting it to 1000ms: yes, that is more or less the animation effect that I am trying to avoid. On a conceptual level, what I am aiming for is (assuming that the user is playing a stream that takes 5-10 seconds to buffer on every seek):

1. Mopidy playback starts and triggers ```playbackStateChanged```, which starts the timer.
2. Instead of progressing the slider immediately, the timer callback starts doing ```mopidy.playback.getTimePosition()``` until the position moves off zero.
3. The callback now starts progressing the slider, and should in theory be in sync with Mopidy's position.

Doing a seek by dragging the slider should work in a similar way as the above:

1. User drags the slider, and  ``ui.value`` (or ``$(this).val()``?) should contain the target position value.
2. ``mopidy.playback.seek(ui.value)`` is called to perform the seek.
3. The timer does the callback and waits for ```mopidy.playback.getTimePosition()``` to match whatever ``ui.value`` returned before progressing the slider.

In practice it doesn't appear to be working at all unfortunately. The results that I am getting with ``mopidy.playback.getTimePosition()`` seem a little unpredictable between track changes (probably because I am not using the promise correctly). So the slider always moves off zero and then glitches back once the track audio actually starts.

> possibly coordinate the naming with whatever we decide for #626

Let's revive this pesky naming discussion. I'd like to coordinate naming for the following four things:
1. Drop full channels (feature maps) -- called spatial dropout [elsewhere](https://github.com/torch/nn/blob/master/doc/simple.md#nn.SpatialDropout)
2. Drop full pixels
3. Softmax across channels, i.e., per pixel -- called spatial softmax [elsewhere](https://github.com/torch/nn/blob/master/doc/transfer.md#spatialsoftmax)
4. Softmax across pixels, i.e., per channel -- called spatial softmax [elsewhere](http://arxiv.org/abs/1509.06113)

*spatial* is a little ambiguous. It tells that it will expect the input to have spatial dimensions and treat them differently, but it doesn't tell in what way.

What about ChannelDropout, PixelDropout, PixelwiseSoftmax, ChannelwiseSoftmax (in that order)?

Close alternatives: PixelwiseDropout does not make clear whether it drops pixels, or has the same dropout mask applied to each pixel. PixelSoftmax does not make clear whether it applies a softmax per pixel, or computes distributions over pixels.

Flaw: While "spatial" can refer to 1D, 2D and 3D (unless in Torch, where it's used for 2D, and volumetric for 3D), "pixel" is pretty much tied to 2D. I wouldn't want to distinguish dimensionalities, though. "location" would be a substitute for "pixel".

Mhm, I started a spatial softmax layer implementation supporting arbitrary axes via shuffling and reshaping: https://github.com/f0k/Lasagne/commit/807763f
Then I noticed that combining this with a cross-entropy that also shuffles and reshapes breaks the optimization: https://github.com/Theano/Theano/issues/5804
This has been fixed in the meantime, but it means the shuffling/reshaping solution won't play well with Theano 0.8.2 or 0.9. I'd still include it in Lasagne, but in the next weeks I don't have any time to finish it, feel free to take over!
Another line of work is updating the Theano softmax implementation to support arbitrary axes: https://github.com/Theano/Theano/pull/5719. This is work in progress, and will be the best solution in the long run.

> Wouldn't the simplest workaround at the moment be something like the following:

Looks good, yes. (If you also want to support integer targets instead of one-hot, you will need some dimshuffling and indexing.)

> If I'm not wrong this should not have stability problems, and the gradient should be automatic. But this way the output is not the softmax, but the logsoftmax... Any way around this?

You can use `nonlinearity=None` for the Conv2DLayer and then put two separate `NonlinearityLayer` on top: One using `algo='log'` that you'd use for the loss function, and one using `algo='accurate'` that you'd use for the prediction function. You might also get away with using `algo='accurate'` for both, with a standard categorical crossentropy, this seems to have worked for at least one user (https://groups.google.com/d/msg/lasagne-users/Ltf_v6V9rTQ/tJAJrasxCQAJ).

Ok, one more thing: there is an inconsistency between PE and ELF formats such that PEs mostly use RVAs internally, which is more convenient in case of rebasing, but ELFs use LVAs. 
As an example - PE sections addresses ([PE:Section Table (Section Headers):VirtualAddress](http://www.osdever.net/documents/PECOFF.pdf)) versus ELF segments addresses ([ELF:Program Header:v_addr](http://www.skyfree.org/linux/references/ELF_Format.pdf)) and also entry points addresses.

Eventually, it can lead to situations when CLE user doesn't know what type of address he have got from CLE, for example, when user requested some information indirectly from pefile/pyelftools via CLE.

What you're looking for is called "actions". There are a ton of different events that are logged over the course of execution, and these end up being collected and logged in a path's history. One of them is a record of exactly where and how a constraint got added to the state.

Because these options take up a lot of memory (they involve holding on to a reference to each intermediate state, I think?) by default we only hold on to the actions for the most recent block. This behavior can be changed by adding the `o.TRACK_ACTION_HISTORY` option to the SimState, but again, memory consumption. The alternative is consuming the actions after every step.

* `%spark config configuration_overrides`

This will save the configuration overrides and every session added after it will start with that configuration.

Thus, add will become:

* `%spark add session_name language conn_string`

Hi @nawalgupta ,
Playback device mimics the real device and should not be dropping frames that are raised from its playback sensors. If you register to each of the playback device's sensors's frame callback then you should get all frames without drops. The pipeline is unaware of the device that is being used and does the same for live and playback device in terms of synchronization (time) and aggregation (to frame set) of frames. 

For an example of how to register for sensor callback please see the rs-sensor-control example and just pass a `playback_device` instance as the `rs2::device` instance (they are polymorphic).
You can then do whatever you wish with the frames, for example synchronize and aggregate the frames using a processing_block (just like the pipeline does). Since the aggregation part is not a public API, we don't have an example that shows how to collect the frames from all sensors and group them into a frameset, you could always look at the pipeline source code but what's the point of doing the same thing that the pipeline does?

We have plans to allow further control over the pipeline (like deciding which processing it should use) but for now, nothing like that is available other than using the device and sensor APIs.

The issue is only on Python 3, this is because in graphene/types/enum.py
there is this:

```
try:                                                                            
    from enum import Enum as PyEnum                                             
except ImportError:                                                             
    from ..pyutils.enum import Enum as PyEnum
```

There seems to actually import again graphene/types/enum.py module, not the enum package from standard lib, as I found out last time I tried to debug this issue.
Try to rename graphene/types/enum.py to something else to see if this resolve the issue.

For a workaround, to not use the wrong imported enum, you can create your enum this way:

```
from graphene.pyutils.enum import Enum as PyEnum
sentiments_enum = PyEnum('SentimentTypes', (                                    
    ('LIKE', 'LIKE'),                                                           
    ('DISAGREE', 'DISAGREE'),                                                   
    ('DONT_UNDERSTAND', 'DONT_UNDERSTAND'),                                     
    ('MORE_INFO', 'MORE_INFO')))                                                
SentimentTypes = graphene.Enum.from_enum(sentiments_enum)
```

I have figured out another (probably ugly) hack, but it works in only 4 lines of code:

    from django.contrib.staticfiles.finders import BaseStorageFinder
    from django.contrib.staticfiles.storage import staticfiles_storage

    class FixedPipelineFinder(BaseStorageFinder):
        storage = staticfiles_storage

Then use this `FixedPipelineFinder` in your settings (I put the finder into a package `hacks.pipeline`):

    STATICFILES_FINDERS = (
        ...
        'hacks.pipeline.FixedPipelineFinder',
    )

The `FixedPipelineFinder` is the same as the `pipeline.finder.PipelineFinder` but with the checks removed from `find` and `list`. Now I can test the pipeline in DEBUG=True using PIPELINE_ENABLED=True in my settings.

OK, this is a fun one.  The problem is with Pyomo - it is asking for signal hangling - but the easiest place to fix this is in PyUtilib.  We don't want to turn off signal handling - when we do, killing Pyomo with a signal (usually Ctrl-C) leaves the solver subprocess running.  I see ~4 options:

1. We could go into every place that Pyomo calls `pyutilib.subprocess.processmngr.run_command` and add try-except traps to automatically disable signal handling - but that is going to be hard to ensure that both we get all of them, and that no new calls sneak in.  It will also be hard to trap: the signals system apparently raises a `ValueError` in this case.  That is a rather common exception, and trapping that to retry without signals 

2. We could add a global `USE_SUBPROCESS_SIGNAL_HANDLERS` flag to Pyomo that defaults to True, but users like @ccocheci could set to False.  The downside is that we would need to pass that global flag to every call to `run_command` (with all the same problems as above)

3. We could add a try-except block into `run_command` itself so that it automatically falls back on disabling signal handling when it cannot set up the signals.  I was going to propose this, but the more I think about it, the less I like it: *bad* things can happen if signals aren't propagated to the subprocess - including leaving solvers running after timelimits.  This would be especially evil for folks running something like Pyomo in a server environment.   think that the user (well, downstream developer) should have to take *positive action* to disable the signal handler.

4. Add a global `SUBPROCESS_USE_SIGNAL_HANDLERS_DEFAULT` (or any other semantically equivalent name) to Pyutilib.  This would change the default value for the run_command.  I think I like this the best:
  a. It requires positive action by the downstream developer to turn off signal handling
  b. It is a single central fix, so we don't have to go update everywhere run_command is called

@whart222:  What do you think of solution 4?  That should be a minimally-invasive change that should make everyone happy.

input_calibration_layer accepts features and applies 1-D calibration per each feature and returns them. In this example, we have 15 features, so the input to this layer has a shape, [batch_size, 15], and then the output should be [batch_size, 15].

Initial output value will depend on hparam's output_min and output_max. Since it is configured to [-1, 1], the output should be a linear function of your input that maps [input_min, input_max] to [-1, 1]. But note that output keypoints are trained jointly with your upper layer DNN. So as training proceeds, the output value will change even for the same input value :).

When a device has registered as a border agent, "joiner-routers" (aka: assisting devices) will send their CoAP-wrapped commissioning traffic to the NCP, which would then send it to the host via  `PROP_THREAD_BA_PROXY_STREAM`. The host could then respond to the "joiner-router" by sending CoAP requests/responses on the stream with the same IPv6 address/port.

Alternatively, we could specify that the address and port is specified using the URI-Host and URI-Port options in the CoAP packet. That would certainly be "cleaner", but it might make the implementation more complicated (since it would require that the packet be mutated by the NCP before being handed up to the host, as well as introspecting into the packet to get the address and port).

We are handling all of this out-of-band (ie: not through the IP stack) because we don't own the RLOC address: that is owned by the NCP alone (although the host driver could certainly expose it to the border-agent process using IPv6, in which case it would be acting like a CoAP proxy. In that case the host driver would do the adding of the URI-Port and URI-Host options, and keeping track of CoAP TID correlation).

Thanks for reporting this. I'd [been wondering](http://anymail.readthedocs.io/en/feature_inbound/esps/mandrill/#inbound-webhook) how Mandrill handled this, as their docs aren't clear about keys for multiple webhooks (and MailChimp [doesn't offer access](https://anymail.readthedocs.io/en/stable/esps/mandrill/) to Mandrill for testing).

I can see three ways to solve this:

1. Switch to a single webhook url for Mandrill, used for both inbound and tracking, and dispatch the inbound or tracking signal based on the event payload. (There shouldn't be a need to introduce a third signal, and doing so would make using Mandrill different from the other ESPs, which is something Anymail tries hard to avoid.)
2. Split the `MANDRILL_WEBHOOK_KEY` setting into separate `MANDRILL_INBOUND_WEBHOOK_KEY` and `MANDRILL_TRACKING_WEBHOOK_KEY` settings.
3. Change `MANDRILL_WEBHOOK_KEY` to allow a list of keys (like [`WEBHOOK_AUTHORIZATION`](https://anymail.readthedocs.io/en/stable/tips/securing_webhooks/#use-a-shared-authorization-secret) does), and validate against any of them.

I'm thinking that even with the first two, we'd *also* want to allow a list of webhook keys, to facilitate credential rotation. (Mandrill generates a new webhook key if you try to change the shared-secret auth part of the webhook url, right?)

Given that, I'd kind of lean toward just doing the third one. Thoughts?

Related question: Have you needed to use Anymail's [`MANDRILL_WEBHOOK_URL`](http://anymail.readthedocs.io/en/stable/esps/mandrill/#std:setting-ANYMAIL_MANDRILL_WEBHOOK_URL) setting? (Were you using the `DJRILL_WEBHOOK_URL` setting?)

So this actually doesn't work, at least not in the way it was intended to.

This code was intended to fix the problem if the client forks. While it doesn't fork anymore (but might in the future), the problem we were having is we registered `atexit_print_messages` to run when the client exits. If the client forks, however, you'd get the messages printed multiple times because the messages would be printed for each exiting process.

Default arguments are evaluated when the function is defined. This allowed us to set the default value of `pid` to the pid of the process that first imported the module. This way, even though all exiting processes called `atexit_print_messages`, only the call that had the matching pid would actually print any messages.

The code proposed in this PR removes this protection though and messages will be printed for every exiting process. There are other solutions though. We could create a global variable or have `atexit_print_messages` register the call with `atexit` itself. In the latter approach, it could set internal state and use it to determine which process causes the messages to printed.

Hopefully this makes sense! Let me know if you have any questions.

Hi @bittner. If you value parity between the travis jobs and the tox envs, then you've pretty much arrived at the necessary setup. If you look at DRF, the [tox conf](https://github.com/encode/django-rest-framework/blob/master/tox.ini) and [travis conf](https://github.com/encode/django-rest-framework/blob/master/.travis.yml) are fairly similar to what you have. There isn't really much to change, except to possibly remove builds for deprecated versions. eg, you could remove the Django 1.7 and 1.9, as well as python 3.3 factors - that would cut down on the travis `exclude` section.

If you don't care about job/env parity, you could remove the django factors from travis, and group envs by python version for each job. For an example of this, look at drf-filters ([tox conf](https://github.com/philipn/django-rest-framework-filters/blob/master/tox.ini), [travis conf](https://github.com/philipn/django-rest-framework-filters/blob/master/.travis.yml)). In this case, all python 3.6 jobs run on the same VM. In addition to the simplicity, the overall build tends to complete faster, as you spend less time setting up additional VMs (~5 VMs instead of ~20).

> Maybe have both the env: section and the matrix: exclude: section be built automagically?

It's certainly possible to write a config generator, but I'm personally not a fan of this approach:
- It's still necessary to verify that the config is correct
- The config would have to cleanly modify an existing file, although this can get messy with manually added/altered includes/exclude sections.

**Updated instructions**

Start Redis

```
rm dump.rdb; ./src/common/thirdparty/redis/src/redis-server --loadmodule src/common/redis_module/ray_redis_module.so
```

Start the global scheduler (and pass in the Redis address)

```
./src/global_scheduler/build/global_scheduler -r 127.0.0.1:6379
```

Start the plasma store

```
src/plasma/build/plasma_store -s /tmp/s1 -m 1000000000
```

Start the plasma manager

```
src/plasma/build/plasma_manager -s /tmp/s1 -m /tmp/m1 -r 127.0.0.1:6379 -h 127.0.0.1 -p 23894
```

Start the local scheduler

```
src/photon/build/photon_scheduler -s /tmp/sched1 -p /tmp/s1 -m /tmp/m1 -r 127.0.0.1:6379 -a 127.0.0.1:23894 -h 127.0.0.1
```

Start a worker (or run this multiple times to start multiple workers).

```
python lib/python/ray/workers/default_worker.py --redis-address 127.0.0.1:6379 --object-store-name /tmp/s1 --object-store-manager-name /tmp/m1 --local-scheduler-name /tmp/sched1 --node-ip-address 127.0.0.1
```

Connect a driver (run this in a Python interpreter).

```python
import ray

address_info = {
  "node_ip_address": "127.0.0.1",
  "redis_address": "127.0.0.1:6379",
  "store_socket_name": "/tmp/s1",
  "manager_socket_name": "/tmp/m1",
  "local_scheduler_socket_name": "/tmp/sched1"}

ray.connect(address_info, mode=ray.SCRIPT_MODE)
```

Some other useful things:
- Start a redis client with `redis-cli` and run `monitor` to monitor all of the commands going through the redis server.

@istoica Hi, Ion, I'm implementing `ray.free()`. From current implementation, the object list passed to `ray.free()` will not be deleted immediately. Each worker will held a PlasmaClient which will hold objects and increase the object ref counts in Object Store. And there is a L3Cache mechanism to delay the PlasmaClient release. The deletion will happen when the ref count is 0. Therefore, we can name the function `ray.garbage_collet()` or `ray.object_store_gc()`, which reminds the user there is a delay in deletion.

Moreover, since these functions are low-level functions, can we put them into another namespace to mark that these functions should be used carefully? For example, we can use `ray.internal.garbage_collet()`.

These connections are closed once the script finishes, though commands are still running:

```
netstat -an | grep 22
tcp        0      0 0.0.0.0:22              0.0.0.0:*               LISTEN     
tcp6       0      0 :::22                   :::*                    LISTEN     
```

```
ps aux | grep sleep | grep -v grep | wc -l
19
```

That is SSH protocol behaviour when not using a PTY. With a PTY, the SSH protocol will terminate any running commands on connection close:

```
output = client.run_command('sleep 10', use_pty=True)
```

```
ps aux | grep sleep | grep -v grep | wc -l
0
```

It's true it would be easier to review as two separate changes. I think they should be mostly orthogonal anyway?

OTOH the big advantage of the "one release of deprecation" model is indeed that it lets you immediately rip out the old stuff. Like discussed in https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/1#issue-202378594, there is a real cost to more complex deprecation processes, and if that's helping our users then that's fine, but we should also be careful not to be making unnecessary work for ourselves. We have no shortage of necessary work :-).

I get why it would make sense to have a minimum waiting period between the release deprecating something and the release removing it. I don't understand why it should also depend on how many releases happen in between – if something is deprecated for, say, a month, then anyone who upgrades during that period will see the warnings, and anyone doesn't, won't. Still working on this. Here are some possibly-incomprehensible notes I scribbled down, so they don't get lost:

Setup:

1. Get a list of all trio fixture objects
1. Mark the topmost ones as depended on by the test
1. Then loop through and mark depended on ones in general. Now every "runnable thing" (fixtures or the test) knows which other runnable things it depends on, and which ones depend on it.
1. Then loop through and start every runnable thing in its own task

For each fixture:

1. Wait for dependencies to be setup
1. If any crashed, abort (and tell reverse dependents to abort)
1. Execute the fixture's setup code. If this raises an exception:

    1. save the error in a list somewhere
    1. mark ourselves as crashed, so our reverse dependents know to abort
    1. mark ourselves as finished tearing down

1. Announce that we're finished setting up (so our reverse-dependents know)
1. Wait for reverse dependents to finish their tear down. If this raises an exception (which can only happen if the fixture opened a nursery/cancel scope – usually this will be because some background task the fixture is running has crashed and triggered the nursery cleanup logic):

    1. Discard `Cancelled` errors; anything else (I guess just `KeyboardInterrupt`?) put into the list
    1. Cancel the test (the actual top-level test)
    1. Resume waiting for reverse dependents, this time with a cancel-scope shield

1. Execute the fixture's teardown code. If this crashes:

     1. Save the error in the list

1. Announce that we're finished tearing down (so our dependents know)

For the test itself:

1. Wait for dependents to be setup
1. If any crashed, abort
1. Set up a cancel scope (so step 5.ii above can cancel it if needed)
1. Run test. If it crashes:

    1. Save the error in the list

1. Tell everyone that we're torn down

After everything has finished: take our list of errors, and raise as MultiError

Replace the current `nursery` fixture with a magic `fixture_nursery` that in each fixture gives you a nursery whose lifetime matches *that fixture*. Implementation: we make a `@pytest_fixture` called `fixture_nursery` that just evaluates to `None` or a sentinel value. Then when evaluating a fixture, if it takes this as an argument, set up a nursery and use it for the `fixture_nursery` argument (instead of the sentinel value). After finished executing the fixture teardown code, but before signalling that teardown is complete, cancel and then exit the nursery. (Why cancel the nursery after teardown, instead of before? The nursery surrounds the actual fixture code, so if we cancelled it before then it would also cancel the teardown code.)

Hi shmulik90,

you are right, the example doesn't work anymore and we found out the reason is the new version of `opencv-python` Python package used for reading JPEG 2000 images from file. 

It was updated 2 days ago from `3.4.0.14` to `3.4.1.15` and now supports OpenCV library `3.4.1`. The new version can only read 8-bit or 16-bit JPEG 2000 files and in case of Sentinel-2 tiles, which are 15-bit, just returns `None`.
(This is actually somewhat improvement from the previous version which read 15-bit incorrectly and we had to write additional code in `sentinelhub` to correct the values.)

So the solution would be that you revert your version of `opencv-python` library to `3.4.0.14` with:
```bash
pip uninstall opencv-python
pip install opencv-python==3.4.0.14
```
And we will update `sentinelhub` setup with dependency to this older version of `python-opencv`. Until OpenCV is improved again or we find alternative library for reading 15-bit jp2 files this solution will have to do.

OK, after a bunch of tinkering around, I still don't really know how the FD is escaping, but I do know that it stops doing it if I change the implementation of `writeFileStream`:

https://github.com/DataBiosphere/toil/blob/024e08525e13fb01aa5d077ef3b1b5def4479503/src/toil/jobStores/fileJobStore.py#L298-L303

If I replace this with an implementation more like `writeFile`, where it creates a file name with `_getUniqueName`, and doesn't rely on coming back after the yield to close the file, I don't seem to leak the file descriptor.

Unfortunately, I was making that change so I would be able to tell where the file was being written from, by putting the caller in the name. So I still have no good reason for the problem, but I do have a solution that I will PR.

pyecharts 不支持 [series[i]-graph.symbolSize](http://echarts.baidu.com/option.html#series-graph.symbolSize) 
可以使用 [series[i]-graph.data[i]](http://echarts.baidu.com/option.html#series-graph.data)

```
nodes: [{
    name: '1',
    x: 10,
    y: 10,
    value: 10
}, {
    name: '2',
    x: 100,
    y: 100,
    value: 20,
    symbolSize: 20,
    itemStyle: {
        color: 'red'
    }
}]
```

一个完整的例子

```
import networkx as nx
from networkx.readwrite import json_graph

from pyecharts import Graph

g = nx.Graph()

g.add_node('N1', name='Node 1', symbolSize=50)
g.add_node('N2', name='Node 2', symbolSize=20)
g.add_node('N3', name='Node 3', symbolSize=30)
g.add_edge('N1', 'N2')
g.add_edge('N1', 'N3')

g_data = json_graph.node_link_data(g)

print(g_data)

eg = Graph('设备最新拓扑图')
eg.add('Devices', nodes=g_data['nodes'], links=g_data['links'],)
print('symbolSize' in eg.options['series'][0])
# eg.show_config()
eg.render()
```

As you said, they're both legal interpretations of your grammar.
Ideally, you should always get one over the other. The inconsistency is a known bug, which we're actively working to fix.
Right now, I can suggest 3 possible solutions:
1) Use rule weights to ensure one if chosen over the other (syntax `rule.weight: ...`)
2) Use the `ambiguity="explicit"` switch to see all possible derivations, and choose the correct one automatically.
3) Switch to LALR(1), which will always match "foo" over CNAME by default.

If you can get away with 3, that's probably your best pick. But 1 & 2 are imho the easiest if you're new to writing grammars.

Hopefully we'll get the fix up and running in the next few weeks (it's a complete re-write of the algorithm).

1. The defaults for all configuration values of all transports and managers are documented.
2. There are only three `Message` attributes with default values, which will be documented shortly.
3. UTF-8 is a sane default, I agree… and it *is* the default.
4. Defaulting to SMTP on `localhost` is dangerous, thus not the default.  It used to be, but was changed around TurboMail 2.0 to prevent accidental message delivery and unintentional spamming.  Because explicit is better than implicit, Marrow Mailer does not assume a default transport or manager.

I don't think it is entirely inaccurate - but I am definitely not proposing we adopt the Message/Message Group terminology and I definitely don't feel strongly about this - let's pretend I didn't bring that up...

If other bindings and the Guide are referring to them as Frames, we should definitely follow that.  I do think we should leave Message there like you have done for backwards compat though.
